Code

Prerequisites

Python 3 installed on your computer
Canvas Personal Access Token
Canvas Course ID

section: Step 1: Get Your Canvas Personal Access Token
text:
Step 1: Get Your Canvas Personal Access Token

Log into Canvas
Go to Account → Settings
Scroll down to Approved Integrations
Click + New Access Token
Give it a purpose (e.g., “STAT 109 Assignment Creation”)
Set an expiration date (optional)
Click Generate Token
Copy the token immediately - you won’t be able to see it again!

Step 2: Find Your Course ID

Go to your STAT 109 course in Canvas
Look at the URL - it will be something like: https://humboldt.instructure.com/courses/12345
The number at the end (e.g., 12345) is your Course ID

Step 3: Install Required Python Packages
pip install requests python-dateutil

Step 4: Configure the Script
Edit create_canvas_assignment.py and update these values at the top:
CANVAS_API_URL = "https://humboldt.instructure.com/api/v1"  # Your Canvas instance
COURSE_ID = "12345"  # Your course ID from Step 2
CANVAS_TOKEN = "your-token-here"  # Your token from Step 1
PUBLIC_SITE_URL = "https://roverhol.github.io/stat109-public"  # Already set correctly
Security Note: For production use, consider storing the token in an environment variable or config file that’s in .gitignore rather than hardcoding it.

Step 5: Test with Lab 1
Run the script to create Lab 1 assignment:
python scripts/create_canvas_assignment.py --type lab --number 1
Or if you want to override the config values:
python scripts/create_canvas_assignment.py --type lab --number 1 --course-id 12345 --token your-token

What the Script Does

Creates an assignment in Canvas with the correct name
Sets the due date based on the schedule
Adds a description with a link to the public course website
Sets the assignment as unpublished by default (you can publish it in Canvas when ready)

Assignment Details Created

Labs

Name: “Lab 1”, “Lab 2”, etc.
Due Date: Tuesday at 11:59 PM (Week 2, 3, 4, etc.)
Submission Type: Online upload and text entry
Link: Points to https://roverhol.github.io/stat109-public/labs/lab-1.html



Quizzes

Name: “Quiz 1”, “Quiz 2”, etc.
Due Date: Thursday at 12:00 PM (beginning of lab)
Submission Type: Online quiz
Link: Points to https://roverhol.github.io/stat109-public/quizzes/quiz-1.html

Next Steps
After creating assignments: 1. Go to Canvas and review the created assignments 2. Adjust points, assignment groups, or other settings as needed 3. Publish assignments when ready 4. Add rubrics or additional instructions in Canvas if needed

Troubleshooting
Error: “Course ID is required” - Make sure you’ve set COURSE_ID in the script or used --course-id
Error: “Canvas token is required” - Make sure you’ve set CANVAS_TOKEN in the script or used --token
Error: 401 Unauthorized - Check that your token is correct and hasn’t expired - Make sure you’re using the correct Canvas API URL
Error: 404 Not Found - Check that your Course ID is correct - Verify the Canvas API URL is correct for your institution

Security Best Practices

Don’t commit your token to git - add scripts/.env or similar to .gitignore
Use environment variables for sensitive data:
import os
CANVAS_TOKEN = os.getenv("CANVAS_TOKEN")
Set token expiration dates when creating tokens
Use minimal permissions - only create tokens with the permissions you need

title: Lab 5
text:
This lab walks you through the planning steps for Project 1. You will state your research question, define \(p\) in context, write your null and alternative hypotheses, and create the graph of the binomial distribution you will use in your project. This is a precheck: you don’t need to have collecting data yet; you are just locking in your question, hypotheses, and the updating values of \(n\) and \(p\) for the R code from Lecture 12 and 13.
What you will turn in: Your research question, a sentence stating what \(p\) represents in context, the direction of your best guess for \(p\) (your alternative hypothesis), and your graph of the binomial distribution (using your \(n\) and \(p\)).



From Project 1, you chose a Binomial Random Process you can observe (with \(n = 25\) trials). It must satisfy the four BRP conditions, and it must not be a class example (e.g., not coin tosses or ESP).
Your turn. In one or two sentences, state the research question you are trying to answer with your process.
My research question:




The parameter \(p\) is the probability of a “success” on a single trial in your process. For your project, “success” might mean a correct guess, a “yes” response, a left turn, etc. — it depends on your question.
Your turn. Write one sentence that states what \(p\) represents in your context (in words, for a reader who doesn’t know your study).
\(p\) in context:




Before observing data, you made a best guess about whether \(p\) is larger than, smaller than, or not equal to some number. That guess is your alternative hypothesis (\(H_a\)). The null hypothesis (\(H_0\)) is the equality that goes with it.
Your turn. Write your null and alternative hypotheses in conventional notation. Use the same number for \(p\) in both (the value under the null).
\(H_0\): \(p\) =
\(H_a\): \(p\) (choose one: \(>\), \(<\), \(\neq\))




The R code in the Lecture 12 / Lecture 13 Colab notebook builds the binomial distribution and plot using \(n\) (number of trials) and \(p\) (probability of success under the null).
Your turn. From your process and \(H_0\), identify the values you will use in the notebook:

\(n\) = (Project 1 uses \(n = 25\) trials.)
\(p\) = (Use the number that appears in \(H_0\).)






Open the Lecture 12 — Binomial Test (R code) Colab notebook (same one used in Lectures 12 and 13).
In the notebook, find where \(n\) and \(p\) are set (in the lecture example they are \(n = 25\) and \(p = 0.2\)).
Change those to your values of \(n\) and \(p\) from Step 4.
Run all the code so that the notebook produces the bar plot of the binomial distribution (the graph of \(P(X = x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, n\)).
Export or save that graph so you can turn it in (e.g., screenshot or download from Colab).

You will update this same graph later for your Project 1 report; for the precheck you only need to submit this graph with your \(n\) and \(p\).




Submit the following in a word or google document to Canvas by Midnight on Tuesday, Feb. 24th:

Research question — The question you wrote in Step 1.
What \(p\) represents — The one-sentence description of \(p\) in context from Step 2.
Direction of your best guess (alternative) — Your \(H_a\) in words or in notation (e.g., “\(p > 0.5\)” or “I think \(p\) is greater than 0.5”).
Your binomial graph — The bar plot of the binomial distribution from Step 5 (using your \(n\) and \(p\)).

This precheck confirms your question, parameter, hypotheses, and graph before you add your data and write the full Project 1 report.

title: Lecture 18
text:
Define the “sampling distribution” of a statistic.
Describe confidence level, parameter, and upper and lower bounds of an interval in the context of an estimation problem.
Compute a confidence interval for a population proportion.
Interpret a confidence interval for a population proportion.
Compute a confidence interval for a population mean.
Interpret a confidence interval for a population mean.





Descriptive statistics summarize the information in a dataset. The goal of inferential statistics is to infer something about a population from a sample.
Definitions: - Population vs sample: We observe information from a subset of people or objects: this set is the sample; in inferential statistics, we want to say something about the population (e.g. all people or objects in our research question), not just the ones in our sample. - Parameter vs statistic: A parameter is a number describing the population: usually this is unknown as we only have information from a subset of the population (a sample); a statistic is a number computed from the sample (e.g. sample proportion, sample mean).
Example: Is Moderna’s vaccine effective?
Moderna vaccine trial (Reuters)
What is the population in this context? The sample? What is the parameter and what is the statistic?




St. Joe’s Hospital in Eureka, California performs a certain heart procedure. We are interested in whether the observed mortality rate at St. Joe’s for this procedure is higher than the national rate.
National mortality rate (benchmark): \(p_0 = 0.08\) (8%).
Two sets of data:

Large sample: 200 patients, with 14 deaths (\(x = 14\), \(n = 200\)).
Small sample: 16 patients, with 3 deaths (\(x = 3\), \(n = 16\)).
Population: All patients who could receive this heart procedure (or all such patients at similar hospitals).
Parameter: The true mortality rate (proportion) at St. Joe’s, often denoted \(\pi\) or \(p\).
Statistic: The proportion who died in the sample: \(\hat{p} = x/n\).





For a binary outcome (e.g. died vs. survived):

Parameter: population proportion \(\pi\) (or \(p\)).
Statistic: sample proportion \(\hat{p}\) = (number of successes) / \(n\).

The sampling distribution of \(\hat{p}\) can be:

Simulated (e.g. using a for loop and mean function in R with a large number of replicates),
Computed exactly using the binomial distribution (e.g. dbinom/pbinom in R),
Approximated with the normal distribution when \(n\) is large enough (e.g. np and n(1-p) are both at least 10).

Use the One Proportion applet to compare the observed sample proportion to the sampling distribution if St. Joe’s had the same mortality rate as the national rate (8%).




For a 95% confidence interval, we use the “2 standard deviations” rule from the Empirical Rule (approximate):
Statistic ± 2 × (standard deviation of statistic)
The “standard deviation of the statistic” is also called the standard error (SE). So:
95% CI ≈ statistic ± 2 × SE
Two important cases:



For a population proportion \(\pi\), we estimate it with the sample proportion \(\hat{p}\). The standard error of \(\hat{p}\) is:
\[\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\]
So the 95% confidence interval for a single proportion is:
\[\hat{p} \pm 2 \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\]
Conditions for Formula 1 (Wald):

Binomial random process: (a) Binary outcome (success/failure), (b) Independent trials, (c) Number of trials \(n\) fixed in advance, (d) Same probability of success \(\pi\) on each trial.
Normal approximation: \(n\hat{p} \geq 10\) and \(n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 10\) (at least 10 successes and 10 failures in the sample, so the sampling distribution of \(\hat{p}\) is approximately normal).





For a population mean \(\mu\), we estimate it with the sample mean \(\bar{x}\). The standard error of \(\bar{x}\) is:
\[\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]
where \(s\) is the sample standard deviation. So the 95% confidence interval for a single mean is:
\[\bar{x} \pm 2 \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]
(For small samples, we often use the \(t\) distribution so the multiplier is slightly larger than 2; here we use 2 for a simple “by hand” rule.)
Conditions for Formula 2:

Either the population is (approximately) normal, or the sample size is \(n > 30\) (so the sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\) is approximately normal by the Central Limit Theorem).
The sample is a random sample (or can be treated as such) from the population.






Goal: Estimate the mortality rate at St. Joe’s (Eureka, CA) for this heart procedure using the large sample (200 patients, 14 deaths), with 95% confidence. The national benchmark is \(p_0 = 0.08\) (8%).

Data: \(n = 200\), \(x = 14\) deaths.
Statistic: \(\hat{p} = 14/200 = 0.07\) (7%).
Standard error: \[\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.07 \times 0.93}{200}} \approx \sqrt{0.0003255} \approx 0.0180.\]
95% CI: \(\hat{p} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\hat{p}) = 0.07 \pm 2(0.0180) = 0.07 \pm 0.036\).
Interval: Lower bound \(\approx 0.034\), upper bound \(\approx 0.106\), so about [0.034, 0.106] or [3.4%, 10.6%].

Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true mortality rate for this heart procedure at St. Joe’s is between about 3.4% and 10.6%. The national rate (8%) lies inside this interval, so the data are consistent with St. Joe’s having the same mortality rate as the national benchmark.




When the sample size is small, the condition for the normal approximation may not be satisfied. Then:

For a 95% confidence interval for a proportion, use the Plus Four formula.
If the confidence level is not 95% and there are at least 10 successes and 10 failures in the sample, you may use the Wald (z) formula. Otherwise, consult a statistician.

Plus Four formula: Add 2 “successes” and 2 “failures” to the sample. With \(x\) = number of successes and \(n\) = sample size, define
\[\tilde{p} = \frac{x+2}{n+4}\]
and use the effective sample size \(n+4\). The standard error of \(\tilde{p}\) is
\[\text{SE}(\tilde{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})}{n+4}}.\]
The 95% confidence interval for a proportion (Plus Four) is:
\[\tilde{p} \pm 2 \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})}{n+4}}.\]
Example (small sample): Estimate the mortality rate at St. Joe’s using the small sample (16 patients, 3 deaths) with 95% confidence. Use the Plus Four method.

Data: \(x = 3\) deaths (successes), \(n = 16\) patients.
Plus Four estimate: \(\tilde{p} = \frac{x+2}{n+4} = \frac{3+2}{16+4} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25\).
Standard error: \(\text{SE}(\tilde{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})}{n+4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.25 \times 0.75}{20}} = \sqrt{0.009375} \approx 0.0968\).
95% CI: \(\tilde{p} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\tilde{p}) = 0.25 \pm 2(0.0968) = 0.25 \pm 0.194\).
Interval: Lower bound \(\approx 0.056\), upper bound \(\approx 0.44\), so about [0.06, 0.44] or [6%, 44%].

Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true mortality rate for this heart procedure at St. Joe’s is between about 6% and 44%. With only 16 patients, the interval is wide and the estimate is uncertain; the Plus Four method gives a plausible range that is more reliable than the Wald interval for such a small sample.




Goal: Estimate the mean recovery time (in days) for patients after the advanced heart procedure using a small sample, with 95% confidence.
Data (recovery time in days for \(n = 5\) patients):
5, 7, 7, 9, 12
By hand:

Sample mean: \(\bar{x} = \frac{5+7+7+9+12}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8\) days.
Sample standard deviation:
Deviations from mean: \(-3\), \(-1\), \(-1\), \(1\), \(4\).
Squared deviations: \(9\), \(1\), \(1\), \(1\), \(16\). Sum = \(28\).
\(s^2 = 28/(5-1) = 7\), so \(s = \sqrt{7} \approx 2.65\) days.
Standard error: \(\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.65}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 1.18\) days.
95% CI: \(\bar{x} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\bar{x}) = 8 \pm 2(1.18) = 8 \pm 2.36\) days.
Interval: Lower bound \(\approx 5.64\) days, upper bound \(\approx 10.36\) days, so about [5.6, 10.4] days.

Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true mean recovery time (in days) for patients after this advanced heart procedure is between about 5.6 and 10.4 days. The 95% confidence means that if we were to repeat the data collection many times, 95% of the time the true mean recovery time would be within the intervals (e.g. lower and upper bounds) computed from the samples. This interval is based on only 5 patients, so it is wide and uncertain; a larger sample would give a narrower and more precise interval.

text:

Statistic: \(\hat{p} = 14/200 = 0.07\) (7%).
Standard error: \[\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.07 \times 0.93}{200}} \approx \sqrt{0.0003255} \approx 0.0180.\]
95% CI: \(\hat{p} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\hat{p}) = 0.07 \pm 2(0.0180) = 0.07 \pm 0.036\).
Interval: Lower bound \(\approx 0.034\), upper bound \(\approx 0.106\), so about [0.034, 0.106] or [3.4%, 10.6%].

Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true mortality rate for this heart procedure at St. Joe’s is between about 3.4% and 10.6%. The national rate (8%) lies inside this interval, so the data are consistent with St. Joe’s having the same mortality rate as the national benchmark.

title: Lecture 18
text:
When the sample size is small, the condition for the normal approximation may not be satisfied. Then:

For a 95% confidence interval for a proportion, use the Plus Four formula.
If the confidence level is not 95% and there are at least 10 successes and 10 failures in the sample, you may use the Wald (z) formula. Otherwise, consult a statistician.

Plus Four formula: Add 2 “successes” and 2 “failures” to the sample. With \(x\) = number of successes and \(n\) = sample size, define
\[\tilde{p} = \frac{x+2}{n+4}\]
and use the effective sample size \(n+4\). The standard error of \(\tilde{p}\) is
\[\text{SE}(\tilde{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})}{n+4}}.\]
The 95% confidence interval for a proportion (Plus Four) is:
\[\tilde{p} \pm 2 \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})}{n+4}}.\]
Example (small sample): Estimate the mortality rate at St. Joe’s using the small sample (16 patients, 3 deaths) with 95% confidence. Use the Plus Four method.

Data: \(x = 3\) deaths (successes), \(n = 16\) patients.
Plus Four estimate: \(\tilde{p} = \frac{x+2}{n+4} = \frac{3+2}{16+4} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25\).
Standard error: \(\text{SE}(\tilde{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})}{n+4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.25 \times 0.75}{20}} = \sqrt{0.009375} \approx 0.0968\).
95% CI: \(\tilde{p} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\tilde{p}) = 0.25 \pm 2(0.0968) = 0.25 \pm 0.194\).
Interval: Lower bound \(\approx 0.056\), upper bound \(\approx 0.44\), so about [0.06, 0.44] or [6%, 44%].

Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true mortality rate for this heart procedure at St. Joe’s is between about 6% and 44%. With only 16 patients, the interval is wide and the estimate is uncertain; the Plus Four method gives a plausible range that is more reliable than the Wald interval for such a small sample.

title: Lecture 18
text:
Goal: Estimate the mean recovery time (in days) for patients after the advanced heart procedure using a small sample, with 95% confidence.
Data (recovery time in days for \(n = 5\) patients):
5, 7, 7, 9, 12
By hand:

Sample mean: \(\bar{x} = \frac{5+7+7+9+12}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8\) days.
Sample standard deviation:
Deviations from mean: \(-3\), \(-1\), \(-1\), \(1\), \(4\).
Squared deviations: \(9\), \(1\), \(1\), \(1\), \(16\). Sum = \(28\).
\(s^2 = 28/(5-1) = 7\), so \(s = \sqrt{7} \approx 2.65\) days.
Standard error: \(\text{SE}(\bar{x}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.65}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 1.18\) days.
95% CI: \(\bar{x} \pm 2 \times \text{SE}(\bar{x}) = 8 \pm 2(1.18) = 8 \pm 2.36\) days.
Interval: Lower bound \(\approx 5.64\) days, upper bound \(\approx 10.36\) days, so about [5.6, 10.4] days.

Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true mean recovery time (in days) for patients after this advanced heart procedure is between about 5.6 and 10.4 days. The 95% confidence means that if we were to repeat the data collection many times, 95% of the time the true mean recovery time would be within the intervals (e.g. lower and upper bounds) computed from the samples. This interval is based on only 5 patients, so it is wide and uncertain; a larger sample would give a narrower and more precise interval.

title: Lecture 15
text:
For a binomial random variable \(X\) with \(n\) trials and success probability \(p\) (\(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)\)):
Mean (expected value): \[\mu = n p\]
Standard deviation: \[\sigma = \sqrt{n\,p\,(1-p)}\]




Mean \(\mu\): The center of the distribution of \(X\). If we observed \(X\) many, many times (each time from a new binomial process with the same \(n\) and \(p\)), the average of those values would be \(\mu = np\).
Standard deviation \(\sigma\): A typical deviation of \(X\) from the center. It is the square root of the average squared distance of (many) observations of \(X\) from the mean \(\mu\). So \(\sigma\) answers: “Roughly how far from \(\mu\) do we expect a single observation of \(X\) to fall?”





For each scenario, (1) state what \(X\) counts and verify the setting is a binomial random process (binary outcome, fixed \(n\), fixed \(p\), independent trials); (2) identify \(n\) and \(p\); (3) compute the mean \(\mu = np\) and the standard deviation \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\).



We run an ESP test with 25 trials. On each trial the person guesses one of 5 symbols; the computer picks one at random. So by chance the probability of a correct guess on any one trial is \(p = 1/5 = 0.2\). Let \(X\) = number of correct guesses out of 25.

\(X\) counts: number of correct guesses in 25 trials.
Binomial? Binary (correct or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p = 0.2\), independent trials. Yes.
\(n\) and \(p\): \(n = 25\), \(p = 0.2\).
Mean: \(\mu = np = 25 \times 0.2 = 5\).
SD: \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{25 \times 0.2 \times 0.8} = \sqrt{4} = 2\).

So we expect about 5 correct by chance, with a typical deviation of about 2.




We take a random sample of 30 students from our school. About 10% of people are left-handed nationwide; we use \(p = 0.10\) as the probability that a randomly chosen student is left-handed. Let \(X\) = number of left-handed students in the sample.

\(X\) counts: number of left-handed students in 30 students.
Binomial? Binary (left-handed or not), fixed \(n = 30\), fixed \(p = 0.10\), independent students. Yes.
\(n\) and \(p\): \(n = 30\), \(p = 0.10\).
Mean: \(\mu = np = 30 \times 0.10 = 3\).
SD: \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{30 \times 0.10 \times 0.90} = \sqrt{2.7} \approx 1.64\).

We expect about 3 left-handed students, with a typical deviation of about 1.64.




A supplier claims that 12% of items are defective. We inspect 20 items at random. Let \(X\) = number of defective items among the 20.

\(X\) counts: number of defective items in 20 inspected.
Binomial? Binary (defective or not), fixed \(n = 20\), fixed \(p = 0.12\) (under the supplier’s claim), independent items. Yes.
\(n\) and \(p\): \(n = 20\), \(p = 0.12\).
Mean: \(\mu = np = 20 \times 0.12 = 2.4\).
SD: \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{20 \times 0.12 \times 0.88} = \sqrt{2.112} \approx 1.45\).

We expect about 2.4 defectives, with a typical deviation of about 1.45.




A basketball player shoots 40 free throws. Her long-run probability of making any single free throw is 0.75. Let \(X\) = number of made free throws out of 40.

\(X\) counts: number of made free throws in 40 attempts.
Binomial? Binary (make or miss), fixed \(n = 40\), fixed \(p = 0.75\), independent shots. Yes.
\(n\) and \(p\): \(n = 40\), \(p = 0.75\).
Mean: \(\mu = np = 40 \times 0.75 = 30\).
SD: \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{40 \times 0.75 \times 0.25} = \sqrt{7.5} \approx 2.74\).

We expect about 30 makes, with a typical deviation of about 2.74.
















Example

\(p\)
\(\mu = np\)
\(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\)

In each case, \(\mu\) is the center of the distribution (the average of many observations of \(X\)), and \(\sigma\) is a typical deviation from that center (square root of the average squared distance from \(\mu\)).

title: Lecture 3
text:
Probability calculations the “long way”



Define and identify the following terms for a given trial:

Sample space
Outcome
Event

Use appropriate notation for the probability of an event, \(P(A)\)
State and use the formula for the probability of an event if the outcomes in the sample space are equally likely.





Let’s define the following terms:

Sample space: the set of all possible outcomes of a trial, conventional notation \(S\)
Event: a set of one or more outcomes of a trial, usually notated with an uppercase letter, e.g. \(A\) or \(B\) or a meaningful letter such as \(H\) for “heads”





Fill in the meaning or notation for each of the following:
Suppose that a single six-sided die is rolled.

\(S\) = __________________________________
Let _____ = be the event that the die lands with the “5” or the “6” side up
Not \(A\) means _______________________________________
Let \(B\) be the event that the die is an even number
\(A\) and \(B\) is the set __________________________________
\(A\) or \(B\) is the set _______________________________________
\(A\) given \(B\) means “assuming that _____________ happened, that the event ________ also happened”





Translate the following “sentences” to English:

\(P(A) = 0.7\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = 0.2\)
\(P(\text{not } A) = 0.1\)
\(P(A|B) = 0.25\)





Fill in the blanks:

For any event \(A\), ____________\(P(A)\) ____________
\(P(S) =\) ____________
For two non-overlapping events \(A\) and \(B\), \(P(A \text{ or } B) =\) ____________


Note: “axiom” is a type of rule that we must merely “agree” is true, rather than being able to “prove” that it is true.

Translate the following sentence to correct probability notation:
“The chance of it raining in the next 10 minutes is 80%”







If all outcomes in the Sample Space \(S\) of a trial are equally likely, then
\[P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|}\]
where \(A\) is any event that is contained in \(S\), and the notation \(|\text{set}|\) is the number of outcomes in the set.




Suppose a fair coin is tossed 2 times.

List the sample space \(S\) of this experiment
List the outcomes in the sample space that are in the event \(B =\) “the coin lands heads at least once”
Find \(P(B)\).











Suppose a fair die is rolled twice.

List the sample space \(S\) of this experiment
List the outcomes in the sample space that are in the event \(C =\) “the sum of the two rolls is 7”
Find \(P(C)\)








Suppose Allen, Beth and Cal are in an elevator, each holding a green lunchbox when an earthquake occurs. In the resulting chaos, their lunch boxes are dropped and shuffled around. Assuming they each pick up one lunch box, find the probability that they each pick up their own lunch box.::: {.content-visible-when-format=“pdf”} :::————————————————————————## Practice Problem: Three Coin TossesSuppose a fair coin is tossed three times. Let \(A\) be the event “the coin lands heads at least once”.Find \(P(A)\).

title: Lecture 14
text:
For each problem, follow these steps:

State the research question.
Define the parameter of interest, \(p\), in words.
State the null and alternative hypotheses using appropriate symbols.
Identify \(n\) and the value of \(p\) under \(H_0\) (for the null distribution).
Verify that the 4 conditions of a Binomial Random Process (BRP) are satisfied by the data collection.
Assuming the null hypothesis is true, create a plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, n, p)\) (the null distribution), e.g. by hand or with software. Do not shade yet — shading will be done by hand in the direction(s) of the alternative.
Identify the observed value of \(x\) from the dataset.
On the plot, add a vertical line at the observed \(x\) and shade the bar(s) in the direction(s) of the inequality in \(H_a\) (by hand).
Compute the p-value: the probability of observing data as or more extreme than the observed \(x\) in the direction of \(H_a\), assuming \(H_0\) is true. (For a two-sided \(H_a\), use both tails.)
Make a conclusion in context (reject \(H_0\) or fail to reject \(H_0\), and what that means for the research question).




Research question: Is the proportion of left-handed students at our school higher than the national rate of 10%?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student from our school is left-handed (i.e., the proportion of left-handers in the population we are sampling).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.10\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.10\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.10\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (left-handed or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Plot: Create a bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.10)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). Leave unshaded; then by hand add a vertical line at \(x = 6\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 6\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.10
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 6 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 5)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(5, 25, 0.10) Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 6\) were left-handed.\(\approx 0.03\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with a 10% left-handed rate; we have evidence that the proportion of left-handed students at our school is higher than 10%.




Research question: Is the proportion of defective items from the new supplier less than 15%?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly selected item from the new supplier is defective (i.e., the proportion of defectives in the process).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.15\) vs \(H_a: p < 0.15\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) items, \(p = 0.15\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (defective or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent items.
Data: In a sample of 25 items, \(x = 3\) were defective.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.15)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 3\) and shade the bars for \(x \leq 3\) (left tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.15
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 3 \mid H_0) = \text{pbinom}(3, 25, 0.15)\) \(\approx 0.47\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 15% defective rate; we do not have evidence that the proportion of defectives from the new supplier is less than 15%.




Research question: Are the two dice fair, in terms of the probability that the sum is 7 or 11? (With fair dice, that probability is \(8/36\): 6 ways to sum to 7 plus 2 ways to sum to 11, out of 36 equally likely outcomes.)
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a single roll of two dice gives a sum of 7 or 11.
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 8/36\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 8/36\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) rolls of the two dice, \(p = 8/36\) (under the null that the dice are fair).
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (sum is 7 or 11, or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent rolls.
Data: In 25 rolls of the two dice, \(x = 2\) times the sum was 7 or 11 (far from the expected \(25 \times (8/36) \approx 5.6\) under \(H_0\)).
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 8/36)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 2\) and shade both tails: bars for \(x \leq 2\) and for \(x \geq 9\) (direction of “more extreme than” for a two-sided alternative). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 8/36
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 2) + P(X \geq 9)\) under \(H_0\). For example, pbinom(2, 25, 8/36) + (1 - pbinom(8, 25, 8/36)) \(\approx 0.04\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with fair dice having probability \(8/36\) for sum 7 or 11; we have evidence that \(p \neq 8/36\).




Research question: Do more than 20% of students at our school use the gym at least once per week?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student uses the gym at least once per week (i.e., the proportion of such students in the population).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.20\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.20\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.20\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (uses gym weekly or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 5\) use the gym at least once per week.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.20)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 5\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 5\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.20
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 5 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 4)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(4, 25, 0.20) \(\approx 0.58\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 20% gym-use rate; we do not have evidence that more than 20% of students use the gym at least once per week.














Example
\(H_a\) direction

title: Lecture 14
text:
Research question: Is the proportion of left-handed students at our school higher than the national rate of 10%?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student from our school is left-handed (i.e., the proportion of left-handers in the population we are sampling).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.10\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.10\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.10\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (left-handed or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Plot: Create a bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.10)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). Leave unshaded; then by hand add a vertical line at \(x = 6\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 6\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.10
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 6 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 5)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(5, 25, 0.10) Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 6\) were left-handed.\(\approx 0.03\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with a 10% left-handed rate; we have evidence that the proportion of left-handed students at our school is higher than 10%.

title: Lecture 14
text:
Research question: Is the proportion of defective items from the new supplier less than 15%?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly selected item from the new supplier is defective (i.e., the proportion of defectives in the process).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.15\) vs \(H_a: p < 0.15\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) items, \(p = 0.15\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (defective or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent items.
Data: In a sample of 25 items, \(x = 3\) were defective.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.15)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 3\) and shade the bars for \(x \leq 3\) (left tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.15
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 3 \mid H_0) = \text{pbinom}(3, 25, 0.15)\) \(\approx 0.47\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 15% defective rate; we do not have evidence that the proportion of defectives from the new supplier is less than 15%.

title: Lecture 14
text:
Research question: Are the two dice fair, in terms of the probability that the sum is 7 or 11? (With fair dice, that probability is \(8/36\): 6 ways to sum to 7 plus 2 ways to sum to 11, out of 36 equally likely outcomes.)
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a single roll of two dice gives a sum of 7 or 11.
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 8/36\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 8/36\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) rolls of the two dice, \(p = 8/36\) (under the null that the dice are fair).
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (sum is 7 or 11, or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent rolls.
Data: In 25 rolls of the two dice, \(x = 2\) times the sum was 7 or 11 (far from the expected \(25 \times (8/36) \approx 5.6\) under \(H_0\)).
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 8/36)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 2\) and shade both tails: bars for \(x \leq 2\) and for \(x \geq 9\) (direction of “more extreme than” for a two-sided alternative). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 8/36
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \leq 2) + P(X \geq 9)\) under \(H_0\). For example, pbinom(2, 25, 8/36) + (1 - pbinom(8, 25, 8/36)) \(\approx 0.04\). Small p-value.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with fair dice having probability \(8/36\) for sum 7 or 11; we have evidence that \(p \neq 8/36\).

title: Lecture 14
text:
Research question: Do more than 20% of students at our school use the gym at least once per week?
Parameter: \(p\) = probability that a randomly chosen student uses the gym at least once per week (i.e., the proportion of such students in the population).
Hypotheses: \(H_0: p = 0.20\) vs \(H_a: p > 0.20\)
\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 25\) students, \(p = 0.20\)
BRP conditions (brief): Binary (uses gym weekly or not), fixed \(n = 25\), fixed \(p\) under null, independent students.
Data: In a sample of 25 students, \(x = 5\) use the gym at least once per week.
Plot: Bar plot of \(x\) vs \(\text{dbinom}(x, 25, 0.20)\) for \(x = 0, 1, \ldots, 25\). By hand, add a vertical line at \(x = 5\) and shade the bars for \(x \geq 5\) (right tail, in the direction of \(H_a\)). Example code to produce the unshaded plot (display only):
n <- 25
p <- 0.20
x_vals <- 0:n
df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = prob)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)")
p-value: \(P(X \geq 5 \mid H_0) = 1 - P(X \leq 4)\). For example, 1 - pbinom(4, 25, 0.20) \(\approx 0.58\). Large p-value.
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a 20% gym-use rate; we do not have evidence that more than 20% of students use the gym at least once per week.

title: Lecture 10
text:
Learning Outcomes:

Define random variable and its probability distribution function (pdf) (for discrete random variables).
Define a binomial random variable and a binomial random process.
Match the notation (\(n\), \(x\), \(p\)) and four conditions for a binomial process with the process of tossing a coin.
Express probabilities for binomial random variables via the binomial pdf formula.




A random variable is defined as…













For a discrete random variable \(X\), the probability distribution function (or probability mass function) gives the probability that \(X\) takes each possible value. We write \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) for each possible value \(x\).









Example 1. A coin is tossed \(n = 1\) time; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) for \(x = 0\) and \(x = 1\).




Example 2. A coin is tossed \(n = 2\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2\).




Example 3. A coin is tossed \(n = 3\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, 3\).




Example 4. A coin is tossed \(n = 4\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\).




General case. A coin is tossed \(n\) times; \(p\) = probability of heads. Find an expression for \(f(x) = P(X = x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\).
.






A binomial random variable \(X\) counts the number of “successes” in a binomial random process.
Four conditions for a binomial random process:

Fixed number of trials \(n\) (e.g., \(n\) coin tosses).
Two outcomes on each trial: “success” or “failure” (e.g., heads vs tails).
Independent trials — the outcome of one trial does not affect the others.
Constant probability \(p\) of success on each trial (e.g., same \(p\) for every toss).

Notation: \(n\) = number of trials, \(p\) = probability of success on one trial, \(X\) = number of successes, and \(x\) = a specific value of \(X\) (0, 1, 2, …, \(n\)).




If \(X\) is binomial with \(n\) trials and success probability \(p\), then for \(x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\),
\[ P(X = x) = f(x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x} = _nC_x\, p^x (1-p)^{n-x} \]
where \(\binom{n}{x} = _nC_x = \dfrac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}\) is the number of ways to choose \(x\) successes out of \(n\) trials.



————————————————————————##

title: Lecture 8
text:
Practice Problem 1. A clinic is testing a new rapid test for COVID on 1000 people. Of these, 20 are known to have COVID and 980 do not. Among those known to have COVID, 17 test positive on the rapid test; among those known to not have COVID, 78 test positive.
Let \(C\) = “has COVID” and \(T^+\) = “tests positive on the new rapid test”

Find \(P(C)\) and \(P(\text{not } C)\) for a randomly selected people from the 1000 people in the study.
Find \(P(T^+ \mid C)\) (sensitivity) and \(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } C)\) (false positive rate).
Find \(P(C \text{ and } T^+)\) and \(P(\text{not } C \text{ and } T^+)\).





Practice Problem 2. Using the COVID data from 1:

If \(C\) and \(T^+\) were independent, what would \(P(C \text{ and } T^+)\) equal?
Is it reasonable to assume that test result (\(T^+\) or \(T^-\)) and COVID status (\(C\) or not \(C\)) are independent? Explain briefly.





Practice Problem 3. In the COVID example from 1, are \(C\) and \(T^+\) actually independent? Explain briefly using the definition of independence.




Practice Problem 4. Using the COVID data from 1, find \(P(T^+)\)




Practice Problem 5. Suppose the prevalence of COVID-19 in a population is \(P(C) = 0.02\) (2%). A rapid test has sensitivity \(P(T^+ \mid C) = 0.85\) and specificity \(P(T^- \mid \text{not } C) = 0.92\) (where \(T^-\) = “tests negative”).

Create tree diagram of the sample space of a person with a disease status and rapid test result, labeling each branch.
What is \(P(T^+ \mid C)\)?
Find \(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } C)\) (the false positive rate).
Find \(P(T^+)\) — the overall probability of a positive test.
Find \(P(C \mid T^+)\) — the probability a person has COVID given they test positive.
Interpret your answer in part (d) in one sentence.
The method of computing \(P(A|B)\) using information from items like $P(B|A) is called Bayes Rule. Match each piece of the calculation in e with the rules shown in Lecture 7.







Practice Problem 6. Recompute \(P(C \mid T^+)\) like in problem 5 but assume the prevalence were \(P(C) = 0.10\) (10%) instead of 2%. How does the result change, and why?

title: Lecture 11
text:
Notation:

\(n\) = number of trials
\(p\) = probability of “success” on a single trial
\(x\) = observed number of successes in \(n\) trials
\(X\) = random variable that counts the number of successes in \(n\) trials

Factorial: \(n! = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(3)(2)(1)\)
Counting:

\(_nC_x = \binom{n}{x} = \dfrac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}\)
\(_nP_x = \dfrac{n!}{(n-x)!}\)

Four conditions for a binomial random process (BRP):

Each trial results in either “success” or “failure”.
Outcomes between trials are independent.
The probability of success \(p\) is the same on every trial.
A fixed number of trials \(n\) are observed.

Binomial random variable: \(X\) = number of successes in \(n\) trials when data are collected from a BRP.
Probability distribution function (pdf) for a binomial random variable \(X\):
\[
f(x) = P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x} = _nC_x\, p^x (1-p)^{n-x}
\]
Cumulative probability: \(P(X \leq x) = f(0) + f(1) + f(2) + \cdots + f(x)\)
Note: \(P(X > x) = 1 - P(X \leq x) = f(x+1) + f(x+2) + \cdots + f(n)\)
Mean of \(X\): \(\mu = np\)
Standard deviation of \(X\): \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\)






Which of the following situations is an example of data being collected from a binomial random process? Be prepared to justify your answer by discussing the validity of each of the four conditions.

Rolling a six-sided die 15 times and counting the number of times a “3” was obtained.
Picking 10 gummy vitamins from a bottle that contains 100 vitamins of various animal shapes. Count the number of lions, tigers, and bears in the set of 10.
Drawing cards one at a time from a deck of 52, counting the number of kings drawn. After each card is drawn, it is replaced in the deck, the deck is shuffled, and another card is drawn.
Drawing cards one at a time from a deck of 52, counting the number of kings drawn. After each card is drawn, it is not replaced in the deck.
Giving a class activity to 20 students and counting the number of these students who ask a question about it during class.








For each situation below, identify \(n\), \(p\), and the appropriate value to compare to \(X\), then write an expression for the probability using the pdf of a binomial random variable.

Problem 2. Suppose Bob flips a fair coin 4 times. What is the probability he gets exactly 2 heads?




Problem 3. Suppose Neal has an unfair coin: it has an 80% chance of landing “heads”. What is the probability that he gets exactly 2 heads if he tosses the coin 4 times?




Problem 4. Suppose that in a week of 7 days, there is a 65% chance of rain on any single day. What is the probability of it raining on at least 2 of the 7 days? On at most 2 of the 7 days?




Problem 5. Suppose customers at a coffee shop can order either iced or hot coffee. Sharon observes 6 customers ordering one day: of these, only 2 order iced coffee. If 10% of all customers order iced coffee, what is the probability of observing 2 or more iced orders (under this model)?




Problem 6. Compute the mean and standard deviation of the random variable \(X\) that is the number of “heads” in 25 tosses of a fair coin.

title: Lecture 4
text:
Lecture 4
Random babies, what are the chances?
The purpose of this activity is to practice to computing a probability using brute force math (a.k.a “careful counting”)
Note: this activity was developed by Allen Rossman and Beth Chance of Cal Poly, SLO. You can see their write-up here.
Story
Suppose that four mothers gave birth to four babies at a hospital and that the we decide to prank the mothers by randomly pairing the babies with mothers.
What is the probability that all four mothers will get their own baby?  Only three mothers? Two? One? None?
Mathematics

List the sample space of the experiment of four babies being randomly returned to their mothers.
Count the number of outcomes that correspond to the event all four mothers get their own baby
Count the total number of ways four babies could be returned to mothers
Compute the probability that all four babies are correctly returned, assuming they are randomly returned so each possible pairing in the sample space is equally likely.
Compare your exact answer in part 4 with the estimate from the simulation shown in class: Random Babies (rossmanchance.com)
Repeat 2 and 4 for the events of exactly 0, 1, 2, or 3 mothers getting the correct baby.

title: Lecture 9
text:
Learning Outcomes:

Distinguish between situations where order matters (permutations) and where order does not matter (combinations).
Use the formulas for \(_nP_r\) and \(_nC_r\) to solve counting problems.




Problem (a). Find the number of ways to distribute 3 distinct flavors of ice cream to 3 distinct bowls.




Problem (b). Find the number of ways to choose 2 distinct flavors of ice cream from 26 flavors to create an ice cream cone with one scoop on the top and one scoop on the bottom.




Problem (c). Find the number of ways to choose 2 distinct flavors of ice cream from 26 flavors to create a dish with two scoops of ice cream, where the order of the scoops doesn’t matter.








Permutation: An arrangement of objects where order matters. (Chocolate on top, vanilla on bottom \(\neq\) vanilla on top, chocolate on bottom.)
Combination: A selection of objects where order does not matter. (Chocolate + vanilla in a dish = vanilla + chocolate.)





The factorial of a nonnegative integer \(n\), written \(n!\), is the product of all positive integers from \(n\) down to 1:
\[
n! = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times 2 \times 1
\]
Convention: \(0! = 1\). (This is defined so that our permutation and combination formulas work correctly when \(r = n\) or \(r = 0\).)
Examples:

\(3! = 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6\)
\(5! = 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 120\)
\(1! = 1\)








Permutations \(_nP_r\) (or \(P(n,r)\)): The number of ways to arrange \(r\) objects chosen from \(n\) distinct objects, where order matters.
\[
_nP_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!} = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-r+1)
\]
Combinations \(_nC_r\) (or \(\binom{n}{r}\) or \(C(n,r)\)): The number of ways to choose \(r\) objects from \(n\) distinct objects, where order does not matter.
\[
_nC_r = \binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} = \frac{_nP_r}{r!}
\]
Relationship: \(_nC_r = \dfrac{_nP_r}{r!}\) — we divide by \(r!\) because each combination of \(r\) objects can be arranged in \(r!\) different orders, and we want to count them once.







Suppose we have 5 letters: A, B, C, D, E. We want to choose 3 of them.
Part 1 (order matters): How many ways can we choose 3 letters if the order of our choice matters? (e.g., for a 3-letter code)?




Part 2 (order doesn’t matter): How many ways can we choose 3 letters if the order of our choice does not matter (e.g., for a committee of 3)?







Rule of thumb: Ask yourself: “If I swap two of the chosen items, do I get a different outcome?” If yes → permutation. If no → combination.

title: Lecture 9
text:
Problem (a). Find the number of ways to distribute 3 distinct flavors of ice cream to 3 distinct bowls.




Problem (b). Find the number of ways to choose 2 distinct flavors of ice cream from 26 flavors to create an ice cream cone with one scoop on the top and one scoop on the bottom.




Problem (c). Find the number of ways to choose 2 distinct flavors of ice cream from 26 flavors to create a dish with two scoops of ice cream, where the order of the scoops doesn’t matter.

title: Lecture 9
text:
Permutation: An arrangement of objects where order matters. (Chocolate on top, vanilla on bottom \(\neq\) vanilla on top, chocolate on bottom.)
Combination: A selection of objects where order does not matter. (Chocolate + vanilla in a dish = vanilla + chocolate.)

title: Lecture 9
text:
The factorial of a nonnegative integer \(n\), written \(n!\), is the product of all positive integers from \(n\) down to 1:
\[
n! = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times 2 \times 1
\]
Convention: \(0! = 1\). (This is defined so that our permutation and combination formulas work correctly when \(r = n\) or \(r = 0\).)
Examples:

\(3! = 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6\)
\(5! = 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 120\)
\(1! = 1\)

title: Lecture 9
text:
Permutations \(_nP_r\) (or \(P(n,r)\)): The number of ways to arrange \(r\) objects chosen from \(n\) distinct objects, where order matters.
\[
_nP_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!} = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-r+1)
\]
Combinations \(_nC_r\) (or \(\binom{n}{r}\) or \(C(n,r)\)): The number of ways to choose \(r\) objects from \(n\) distinct objects, where order does not matter.
\[
_nC_r = \binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} = \frac{_nP_r}{r!}
\]
Relationship: \(_nC_r = \dfrac{_nP_r}{r!}\) — we divide by \(r!\) because each combination of \(r\) objects can be arranged in \(r!\) different orders, and we want to count them once.

title: Lecture 9
text:
Suppose we have 5 letters: A, B, C, D, E. We want to choose 3 of them.
Part 1 (order matters): How many ways can we choose 3 letters if the order of our choice matters? (e.g., for a 3-letter code)?




Part 2 (order doesn’t matter): How many ways can we choose 3 letters if the order of our choice does not matter (e.g., for a committee of 3)?

title: Lecture 9
text: Rule of thumb: Ask yourself: “If I swap two of the chosen items, do I get a different outcome?” If yes → permutation. If no → combination.

title: Quiz 4 — Solutions
text:
In words, what does \(P(A \mid B)\) mean?
State the formula for \(P(A \mid B)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\)).

Solution:

\(P(A \mid B)\) means the probability that event \(A\) occurs given that event \(B\) has occurred.
\(P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}\)






State the multiplication rule for \(P(A \text{ and } B)\) in terms of \(P(A \mid B)\) and \(P(B)\).
State the special case when \(A\) and \(B\) are independent.

Solution:

\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B)\) (or equivalently \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)\))
When \(A\) and \(B\) are independent: \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)






In words, when are two events \(A\) and \(B\) independent?
Give two equivalent ways to check independence (formulas or conditions).

Solution:

Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other.
Here are three equivalent ways you just need to give two: \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\)), or \(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\) (when \(P(A) > 0\)), or \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)





Write a one-sentence interpretation for each:

\(P(A \mid B) = 0.3\)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)

Solution:

Given that \(B\) occurs, the probability that \(A\) occurs is 0.3 (or: there is a 30% chance of \(A\) given \(B\)).
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent.





Solution:

\(P(A \text{ and } B) \to\) (c)
\(P(A) \to\) (a)
\(P(A \mid B) \to\) (b)
\(P(B \mid A) \to\) (d)
\(P(A \text{ or } B) \to\) (e)





A fair six-sided die is rolled once.

Find \(P(\text{roll is 2} \mid \text{roll is even})\).
Find \(P(\text{roll is even} \mid \text{roll is 2})\).

Solution:

\(P(\text{2} \mid \text{even}) = \dfrac{P(\text{2 and even})}{P(\text{even})} = \dfrac{P(\text{2})}{P(\text{even})} = \dfrac{1/6}{3/6} = \dfrac{1}{3}\)
\(P(\text{even} \mid \text{2}) = \dfrac{P(\text{even and 2})}{P(\text{2})} = \dfrac{P(\text{2})}{P(\text{2})} = 1\) (since 2 is even)









Solution:

\(P(A) = \dfrac{6}{10} = \dfrac{3}{5}\)
\(P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{5}{9}\) (5 red remain out of 9 candies)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A) = \dfrac{5}{9} \cdot \dfrac{6}{10} = \dfrac{30}{90} = \dfrac{1}{3}\)





Solution:
\[
P(\text{blue first and green second}) = P(\text{green second} \mid \text{blue first})\,P(\text{blue first}) = \frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{4}{8} = \frac{16}{56} = \frac{2}{7}
\]




Solution:
Yes, they are independent.

\(P(\text{red}) = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}\), \(P(\text{round}) = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}\)
\(P(\text{red and round}) = \dfrac{1}{6}\) (one red round tile)
\(P(\text{red})\,P(\text{round}) = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{6} = P(\text{red and round})\)

Since \(P(\text{red and round}) = P(\text{red})\,P(\text{round})\), the events are independent.




Solution:
\[
P(\text{H first and T second}) = P(\text{H})\,P(\text{T}) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}
\]
(The two tosses are independent.)




Solution:
\[
P(\text{second is green} \mid \text{first is blue}) = \frac{4}{7}
\]
(After removing one blue marble, 4 green and 3 blue remain.)




Solution:

Yes, \(A\) and \(B\) are independent. The first and second rolls are independent trials; knowing the first roll is even does not change the probability that the second roll is 6.
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B) = \dfrac{3}{6} \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{3}{36} = \dfrac{1}{12}\)






COVID Setup: A clinic tests a new rapid test for COVID on 1000 people. Of these, 20 have COVID and 980 do not. Among those with COVID, 17 test positive; among those without COVID, 78 test positive.



Solution:

\(P(C) = \dfrac{20}{1000} = 0.02\), \(P(\text{not } C) = \dfrac{980}{1000} = 0.98\)
\(P(T^+ \mid C) = \dfrac{17}{20} = 0.85\) (sensitivity)
\(P(T^+ \mid \text{not } C) = \dfrac{78}{980} \approx 0.0796\) (false positive rate)





Solution:

\(P(C \text{ and } T^+) = \dfrac{17}{1000} = 0.017\)
\(P(\text{not } C \text{ and } T^+) = \dfrac{78}{1000} = 0.078\)





Solution:

If \(C\) and \(T^+\) were independent: \(P(C \text{ and } T^+) = P(C)\,P(T^+)\). From C4, \(P(T^+) = 0.095\), so this would equal \(0.02 \times 0.095 = 0.0019\).
No, they are not independent. The actual \(P(C \text{ and } T^+) = 0.017\), which is much larger than \(0.0019\). Knowing the test result changes the probability of having COVID (and vice versa).





Solution:
\[
P(T^+) = P(C \text{ and } T^+) + P(\text{not } C \text{ and } T^+) = \frac{17}{1000} + \frac{78}{1000} = \frac{95}{1000} = 0.095
\]




Solution:
\(P(T^+)\) is the overall proportion of people in this study who tested positive on the rapid test (95 out of 1000, or 9.5%).








Solution:
balls <- rep(c("R", "W"), c(5, 2))




Solution:
balls <- rep(c("R", "W"), c(3, 3))
replicates <- 10000
successes <- vector("numeric", replicates)
set.seed(2018)
for (k in 1:replicates) {
  draw <- sample(balls, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  if (draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R") {
    successes[k] <- 1
  }
}
mean(successes)




Solution:
which(successes == 1)




Solution:
Cross out: draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R"
Replace with: (draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R") | (draw[1] == "R" & draw[2] == "W")
(Or equivalently: sum(draw == "R") == 1)




Solution:
eggnog <- sample(c(1, 0), size = 10000, prob = c(0.7, 0.3), replace = TRUE)




Solution:
sum(disease == 1 & test_result == 1) / 10000
(Or: mean(disease == 1 & test_result == 1))

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
text: This notebook reviews new R functions since Lab 4, summarizes how to do a one-proportion test by hand (Lecture 16), and shows two examples: one where the normal approximation is poor and one where it is good.

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
text:
1. New R functions since Lab 4








Function
Package
Purpose




geom_line()
ggplot2
Draws a line connecting points (e.g. a curve). Use for the normal density curve.


dnorm(x, mean, sd)
base R
Normal density at x with given mean and standard deviation. Returns the height of the bell curve at x. Use with a sequence of x values to plot the full curve via geom_line().



Typical use: Create a data frame with a fine grid of x and y = dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma), then ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + geom_line() to draw the bell curve. Same x-axis scale as the binomial bars so you can overlay or compare.

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
section: 2. How to do a one-proportion test by hand (Lecture 16)
text:
2. How to do a one-proportion test by hand (Lecture 16)

Find \(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\) (number of trials and the proportion you are testing).
Check the condition: \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\). If both hold, the normal approximation is reasonable; if not, the binomial is skewed and the normal fit is poor.
Draw the bell curve: Sketch a normal curve (or plot it) with mean \(\mu = np\) and spread (standard deviation) \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\).
Mark the mean and spread: Label \(\mu\) and (optionally) \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\), \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\) on the axis.
Locate the observed value \(x\) on the horizontal axis.
Decision rule: If the observed value is within 2 SDs of the mean (between \(\mu - 2\sigma\) and \(\mu + 2\sigma\)) → keep \(H_0\) (fail to reject). If it is beyond 2 SDs (in either tail) → reject \(H_0\).

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
text:
Setup


Code
library(ggplot2)

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
text:
Example 1: Poor normal approximation (small \(n\))
Setup: \(n = 10\), \(p = 0.5\) (e.g. 10 coin flips, testing fairness). Then \(np = 5\) and \(n(1-p) = 5\), so both are \(< 10\) → the normal approximation is poor; the binomial is chunky and the bell curve doesn’t fit well.
We plot the binomial bars (dbinom + geom_col) and overlay the normal curve (dnorm + geom_line) on the same x-axis, with vertical lines at \(\mu\) and \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\), \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\).


Code
# Parameters
n <- 10
p <- 0.5
mu <- n * p
sigma <- sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
cat("n =", n, "  p =", p, "  mu =", mu, "  sigma =", round(sigma, 4), "\n")
cat("np >= 10?", n*p >= 10, "  n(1-p) >= 10?", n*(1-p) >= 10, "  => approximation is POOR\n")




Code
# Binomial: bars (dbinom + geom_col)
x_vals <- 0:n
df_binom <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))

# Normal: curve (dnorm + geom_line)
x_fine <- seq(0, n, by = 0.05)
df_norm <- data.frame(x = x_fine, y = dnorm(x_fine, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

# Overlay: bars first, then line
ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob), fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(data = df_norm, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.2, color = "darkred") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red", linetype = "solid") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 5) +
  annotate("text", x = mu - sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.75, label = "μ−σ", color = "orange", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = mu + sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.75, label = "μ+σ", color = "orange", size = 3.5) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "probability / density", title = "Example 1: Poor approximation (n=10, p=0.5)") +
  theme_minimal()


Takeaway: The binomial bars are not well covered by the red normal curve — the distribution is too discrete and the normal doesn’t match the skew. So we would not rely on the normal approximation here; use the exact binomial (e.g. pbinom) for the test.

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
text:
Example 2: Good normal approximation (larger \(n\))
Setup: \(n = 50\), \(p = 0.4\). Then \(np = 20\) and \(n(1-p) = 30\), so both \(\geq 10\) → the normal approximation is good; the binomial bars sit nicely under the bell.
Same plot: binomial bars + normal curve + mean and \(\pm 1\sigma\), \(\pm 2\sigma\).


Code
# Parameters
n <- 50
p <- 0.4
mu <- n * p
sigma <- sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
cat("n =", n, "  p =", p, "  mu =", mu, "  sigma =", round(sigma, 4), "\n")
cat("np >= 10?", n*p >= 10, "  n(1-p) >= 10?", n*(1-p) >= 10, "  => approximation is GOOD\n")




Code
# Binomial: bars
x_vals <- 0:n
df_binom <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))

# Normal: curve (dnorm + geom_line)
x_fine <- seq(0, n, by = 0.1)
df_norm <- data.frame(x = x_fine, y = dnorm(x_fine, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

# Overlay
ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob), fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(data = df_norm, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.2, color = "darkred") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red", linetype = "solid") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 5) +
  annotate("text", x = mu - sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.8, label = "μ−σ", color = "orange", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = mu + sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.8, label = "μ+σ", color = "orange", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = mu - 2*sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.65, label = "μ−2σ", color = "purple", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = mu + 2*sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.65, label = "μ+2σ", color = "purple", size = 3.5) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "probability / density", title = "Example 2: Good approximation (n=50, p=0.4)") +
  theme_minimal()


Takeaway: The binomial bars sit closely under the red normal curve. So we can use the normal approximation for the test: draw the bell with mean and spread, mark the observed value, and apply the rule — within 2 SDs of the mean ⇒ keep \(H_0\); beyond 2 SDs ⇒ reject \(H_0\).

title: Demo: Binomial vs Normal Approximation
text:
Summary











Example
\(n\)
\(p\)
\(np\), \(n(1-p)\)
Condition
Approximation




1
10
0.5
5, 5
Both \(< 10\)
Poor — binomial chunky, normal doesn’t fit


2
50
0.4
20, 30
Both \(\geq 10\)
Good — bars follow the bell



R: Use geom_line() with a data frame of dnorm(x, mean, sd) to plot the normal curve on the same x-axis as the binomial bars from dbinom + geom_col().

title: Demo: New R Functions for Lab 3
text: Purpose: A short demo of the new functions you’ll use for conditional probability and simulation. Only two probability examples — drink/chocolate and balls in a bag — and a clear use of if and which for conditional counting.

title: Demo: New R Functions for Lab 3
text:
Summary of New Functions








Function / Operator
What it does
Example use




rep(x, times)
Repeat elements of a vector
rep(c("R", "W"), c(3, 3)) → 3 red, 3 white balls


table(x)
Count how often each value appears
table(drink) → how many tea vs coffee


table(x, y)
Cross-tabulate two variables
table(drink, chocolate) → counts by drink and chocolate


addmargins(table(x, y))
Add row and column totals to a table
See totals at a glance


which(condition)
Return indices where condition is TRUE
which(successes == 1) → which runs succeeded


&
Logical AND (both must be true)
draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R"


|
Logical OR (at least one true)
combine two outcomes in one condition


if (condition) { ... }
Run code only when condition is TRUE
Count successes in a loop (conditional on the draw)

title: Demo: New R Functions for Lab 3
section: Example 1: Drink and chocolate (one variable conditional on another)
text:
Example 1: Drink and chocolate (one variable conditional on another)
Simulate 50 people: 50% prefer tea / 50% coffee. Chocolate preference (milk vs dark) depends on drink — tea drinkers lean milk, coffee drinkers lean dark. We use if to assign chocolate with the right probabilities for each person.


Code
set.seed(109)
n <- 50
drink <- sample(c("tea", "coffee"), size = n, replace = TRUE)
chocolate <- vector("character", n)

for (k in 1:n) {
  if (drink[k] == "tea") {
    chocolate[k] <- sample(c("milk", "dark"), size = 1, prob = c(0.7, 0.3), replace = TRUE)
  }
  if (drink[k] == "coffee") {
    chocolate[k] <- sample(c("milk", "dark"), size = 1, prob = c(0.3, 0.7), replace = TRUE)
  }
}

# One variable: table(x)
table(drink)

# Two variables: table(x, y)
table(drink, chocolate)

# Add totals: addmargins
addmargins(table(drink, chocolate))


Exact probabilities (Example 1): \(P(\text{tea}) = P(\text{coffee}) = 0.5\). \(P(\text{dark}|\text{tea}) = 0.3\), \(P(\text{dark}|\text{coffee}) = 0.7\). So \(P(\text{tea and dark}) = (0.3)(0.5) = 0.15\), \(P(\text{coffee and dark}) = (0.7)(0.5) = 0.35\), and \(P(\text{dark}) = 0.15 + 0.35 = 0.50\).

title: Demo: New R Functions for Lab 3
section: Example 2: Balls in a bag — rep, if, and which for conditional success
text:
Example 2: Balls in a bag — rep, if, and which for conditional success
Bag: 3 red, 3 white. We build it with rep(c("R", "W"), c(3, 3)) — repeat “R” 3 times and “W” 3 times. Draw 2 balls without replacement. We want to estimate \(P(\text{white first and red second})\).

if: Inside the loop, we only record a success when the condition holds. So we assign a 1 to successes[k] only when the first ball is white and the second is red (&).
which: After the loop, which replicates were successes? which(successes == 1) tells us the run numbers where we got white then red.
|: To count “exactly one red” we’d use (W then R) or (R then W); the logical or is |.



Code
set.seed(99)
balls <- rep(c("R", "W"), c(3, 3))
replicates <- 20
successes <- vector("numeric", replicates)

for (k in 1:replicates) {
  draw <- sample(balls, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  if (draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R") {
    successes[k] <- 1
  }
}

successes
which(successes == 1)
mean(successes)


Exact probability (Example 2): \(P(W_1 \text{ and } R_2) = P(R_2|W_1)\,P(W_1) = (3/5)(1/2) = 3/10 = 0.3\). With more replicates, mean(successes) will be close to 0.3.
Using | for “exactly one red”: We could count successes when (W then R) or (R then W) by changing the condition to: if ((draw[1] == "W" & draw[2] == "R") | (draw[1] == "R" & draw[2] == "W")) — then which(successes == 1) shows which runs had exactly one red.

title: Demo: New R Functions for Lab 3
text:
Diagnostic test (conditional probability)
Suppose a disease has prevalence \(P(D) = 0.01\). A test has sensitivity \(P(\text{positive}|D) = 0.95\) and specificity \(P(\text{negative}|\text{no } D) = 0.90\).
our task: Simulate 10,000 individuals with disease status and test result. Estimate \(P(D|\text{positive})\) — the probability of having the disease given a positive test.


Code
p_disease <- 0.01
p_pos_given_disease <- 0.95
p_neg_given_no_disease <- 0.90
n <- 10000

disease <- vector("numeric", n)
test_result <- vector("numeric", n)  # 0 = negative, 1 = positive

set.seed(109)

# Assign disease status
disease <- sample(c(0, 1), size = n, prob = c(1 - p_disease, p_disease), replace = TRUE)

# Assign test result (conditional on disease)
for (k in 1:n) {
  if (disease[k] == 1) {
    test_result[k] <- sample(c(0, 1), size = 1, 
                            prob = c(1 - p_pos_given_disease, p_pos_given_disease), replace = TRUE)
  }
  if (disease[k] == 0) {
    test_result[k] <- sample(c(0, 1), size = 1, 
                            prob = c(p_neg_given_no_disease, 1 - p_neg_given_no_disease), replace = TRUE)
  }
}

table(disease, test_result)

# P(D | positive) = P(D and positive) / P(positive)
n_pos <- sum(test_result == 1)
n_disease_and_pos <- sum(disease == 1 & test_result == 1)
n_disease_and_pos / n_pos


Exact answer (definition of conditional probability, multiplication rule, and addition rule for non-overlapping events):
By the definition of conditional probability: \(P(D|\text{pos}) = P(D \text{ and pos}) / P(\text{pos})\).
By the multiplication rule: \(P(D \text{ and pos}) = P(\text{pos}|D)\,P(D) = (0.95)(0.01) = 0.0095\).
Find \(P(\text{pos})\) by splitting into two non-overlapping ways to get a positive: (pos and D) or (pos and no D). By the addition rule for non-overlapping events: \(P(\text{pos}) = P(\text{pos and } D) + P(\text{pos and no } D)\). Use the multiplication rule for each: \[P(\text{pos}) = P(\text{pos}|D)\,P(D) + P(\text{pos}|\text{no }D)\,P(\text{no }D) = (0.95)(0.01) + (0.10)(0.99) = 0.0095 + 0.099 = 0.1085\]
So: \[P(D|\text{pos}) = \frac{P(D \text{ and pos})}{P(\text{pos})} = \frac{0.0095}{0.1085} \approx 0.0876\]
So about 8.8% — even with a positive test, the probability of having the disease is low when the disease is rare. The simulation should give about 0.09.

Step 1 — Create the repo (1 hour)

Copy your existing Quarto+GitHub Actions template repo.

Change title/theme for STAT109.

Create folders exactly as in the structure above.

Make sure GitHub Pages deployment works (one test push).

Step 2 — Make the three “canonical maps” (2–3 hours)

Create concepts/learning-objectives.yml (IDs + text).

Create methods.qmd (convert your 2-page table; add anchors).

Create r-reference.qmd (paste base-R cheat sheet; add anchors).

Step 3 — Build your lesson template (30 min)

Create one file lessons/L00-template.qmd (not linked on site).

Include the flow sections: Physical → Simulation → Math+Conditions → Hand → R → Practice.

Step 4 — Convert Weeks 1–3 (most important) (one focused day)

For each of the first 3 lecture outlines:

Convert OneNote outline → lessons/L01.qmd, L02.qmd, L03.qmd.

Keep content concise; preserve whitespace for work.

Add a “Practice” list with problem IDs (even if those problems aren’t converted yet).

Convert 10–15 practice problems into problems/ with IDs + LO tags.

Convert solutions for the 5–8 you’ll reuse most soon.

Create quizzes/QZ-01.qmd listing problem IDs.

Step 6 — Convert Lab 1 into a notebook (2–4 hours)

Take your R script → labs/LAB-01.qmd.

Add narrative prompts between chunks.

Link it from Lesson 1 and/or Schedule.

Step 7 — Projects: export + publish Project 1 (2–3 hours)

Export the Canvas rubric for Project 1 into projects/raw/.

Create projects/PRJ-01.qmd and projects/PRJ-01-rubric.qmd.

Put only the submission link in Canvas; keep everything else on the site.

Step 8 — Build the schedule hub (1–2 hours)

In schedule.qmd, create a table for Weeks 1–15.

Each week links to: lesson(s), lab, quiz, project (when applicable), readings.

title: Assessments
text:
This course includes several types of assessments designed to help you learn and to demonstrate your understanding of statistical concepts and methods.








Component
Weight
Description




Daily Participation
5%
Warm-up problems in lecture (Mondays, Wednesdays, Fridays)


Weekly quizzes
30%
Quizzes on Thursdays at the beginning of lab


Weekly labs
15%
Programming labs due Tuesdays in Canvas


Two midterms
30%
Midterm 1 (Week 7, Friday) and Midterm 2 (Week 13, Friday)


Five projects
20%
Projects due Sundays at ends of weeks 5, 9, 11, 12, and 15


Total
100%







Daily warm-up problems are completed at the beginning of each lecture (Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays). These collaborative activities give you an opportunity to work as a team with your classmates and practice key concepts.



Weekly programming labs provide hands-on practice with statistical analysis using R. Labs are due Tuesdays in Canvas. I’ll help you write the code during the Thursday lab session before they’re due. Labs are graded on completion and your reflection. After the due date, a solution will be posted, so late submissions are generally not accepted except in documented emergencies.



Lab 1 - Week 1 (due Tuesday, Week 2)
Lab 2 - Week 2 (due Tuesday, Week 3)
Lab 3 - Week 3 (due Tuesday, Week 4)
Lab 4 - Week 4 (due Tuesday, Week 5)
Lab 5 - Week 5 (due Tuesday, Week 6)
Lab 6 - Week 6 (due Tuesday, Week 7)
Lab 7 - Week 8 (due Tuesday, Week 9)
Lab 8 - Week 9 (due Tuesday, Week 10)
Lab 9 - Week 10 (due Tuesday, Week 11)
Lab 10 - Week 11 (due Tuesday, Week 12)
Lab 11 - Week 12 (due Tuesday, Week 13)
Lab 12 - Week 14 (due Tuesday, Week 15)
Lab 13 - Week 15 (due Tuesday, Finals Week)





Weekly quizzes are administered on Thursdays at the beginning of lab. They assess your understanding of key concepts from the lessons, practice problems, and labs. If you need to miss a quiz due to illness or other emergencies, please contact me to make an arrangement to take it at another time.



Quiz 1 - Week 1
Quiz 2 - Week 2
Quiz 3 - Week 3
Quiz 4 - Week 4
Quiz 5 - Week 5
Quiz 6 - Week 6
Quiz 7 - Week 8
Quiz 8 - Week 9
Quiz 9 - Week 10
Quiz 10 - Week 11
Quiz 11 - Week 12
Quiz 12 - Week 14
Quiz 13 - Week 15





Five projects throughout the semester allow you to apply statistical methods to practical, real-world problems. Projects are due on Sundays at the ends of weeks 5, 9, 11, 12, and 15 in Canvas. You may submit a late project up to a week late with no penalty. After a week, a project can be turned in for 70% of the grade. Projects can be used as part of a portfolio.



Project 1 - Due Sunday, Week 5 (Feb 22)
Project 2 - Due Sunday, Week 9 (Mar 29)
Project 3 - Due Sunday, Week 11 (Apr 12)
Project 4 - Due Sunday, Week 12 (Apr 19)
Project 5 - Due Sunday, Week 15 (May 10)








Midterm 1: Week 7, Friday (in lecture)
Midterm 2: Week 13, Friday (in lecture)

Midterms require you to demonstrate your understanding of the course material. If you need to miss a midterm due to illness or other emergencies, please communicate with me as soon as possible to reschedule it. Alternatively, you may take the optional final exam to replace a missed midterm grade.

title: Quiz 1
text:
Quiz 1
This quiz will be held in the first 10 minutes of lab on Thursday. It will contain two questions:

What is the definition of Statistics?
What are the five steps in the Statistical Process?
