title: Lecture 17
text:
What is the probability of making a mistake in a test?



Define Type I and Type II errors in terms of the null and alternative hypotheses in a test.
Describe the result of either a Type I or Type II error in the context of a specific research question.
Define power both in terms of the null and alternative hypotheses in a test and as the opposite of the probability of making a Type II error.
Balance the two types of error in a test by:

using the consequence of a Type I error to inform the choice of the level of significance (e.g. how small the p-value needs to be to reject the null hypothesis), and
increasing the sample size until a reasonable value for power / probability of a Type II error is achieved (assuming a particular alternative hypothesis is true).






Hypothesis testing works like a trial by jury:

Null hypothesis (\(H_0\)) = the default assumption of innocence. We assume “no effect” or “no difference” until there is strong evidence otherwise.
P-value = the weight of the evidence against the null. A small p-value means the data would be unlikely if \(H_0\) were true — strong evidence against “innocence.” A large p-value means the data are plausible under \(H_0\) — we do not have enough evidence to reject the default.
Reject \(H_0\) = like a guilty verdict: we conclude the evidence is strong enough to reject the default (innocence).
Fail to reject \(H_0\) = like a not-guilty verdict: we do not have enough evidence to reject the default; we are not saying \(H_0\) is “true,” only that we cannot reject it.





The usual flow:

State the hypotheses: \(H_0\) (null, default) and \(H_a\) (alternative).
Collect data and compute a test statistic (e.g. sample proportion, count).
Compute the p-value: the probability of observing data as or more extreme than what we saw, if \(H_0\) were true.
Decide: If the p-value is small (e.g. below a chosen significance level \(\alpha\)), reject \(H_0\); otherwise fail to reject \(H_0\).

Every decision is made in the face of uncertainty: \(H_0\) might really be true or false, and we might reject it or not. So we can make mistaken decisions.




Our decision (reject or fail to reject \(H_0\)) can be right or wrong depending on reality (whether \(H_0\) is actually true or false):









Reality: \(H_0\) true
Reality: \(H_0\) false




Decision: Reject \(H_0\)
Type I error (false alarm)
Correct — we reject when we should (power)


Decision: Fail to reject \(H_0\)
Correct — we keep the default when it’s true
Type II error (missed opportunity)




Type I error: Rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true.
Type II error: Failing to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually false.
Power: Probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. So Power = 1 − P(Type II error).






Type I error is a “false alarm”: we conclude there is an effect (reject the null) when there isn’t. In the trial analogy, we convict an innocent person.
Type II error is a “missed opportunity”: we fail to conclude there is an effect when there really is one. In the trial analogy, we fail to convict someone who is guilty.

We cannot make both error probabilities arbitrarily small at once with a fixed sample size. So we balance them: choose a significance level \(\alpha\) (max acceptable Type I error) and then try to achieve reasonable power (low Type II error) via sample size or design.





Type I error: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true. We control this with the significance level \(\alpha\) (e.g. \(\alpha = 0.05\)): we reject \(H_0\) only when the p-value \(< \alpha\).
Type II error: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false. Its probability depends on the true state of nature (e.g. how far the true \(p\) is from the null value), the sample size, and \(\alpha\).
Power: The probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. Power = 1 − P(Type II error). Higher power means we are more likely to detect a real effect when it exists.






When Type I error is more serious (e.g. approving a harmful drug): use a smaller \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.01) so we require stronger evidence (smaller p-value) before rejecting \(H_0\).
When Type II error is more serious (e.g. missing a beneficial treatment): we want higher power. We can increase power by increasing the sample size or, if acceptable, using a somewhat larger \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.10).
Increasing sample size generally increases power (reduces Type II error) without changing the Type I error rate \(\alpha\).





Setup: We want to know if a coin lands on heads more often than not (a “lucky” coin for heads).
State the hypotheses

\(H_0\): The coin is fair; \(p = 0.5\) (probability of heads).
\(H_a\): The coin is lucky for heads; \(p > 0.5\).

Define errors in context

Type I error: Concluding that the coin is “lucky” when it actually is not (false alarm).
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the coin is “lucky” when it actually is (missed opportunity).

Define power

Power = probability of correctly concluding the coin is lucky when it really is (\(p > 0.5\)); i.e. Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

Balance the errors

Choose \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.05) to control how often we falsely declare the coin lucky. To reduce the chance of missing a truly lucky coin, increase the number of flips (sample size) to increase power.





Setup: We want to know if a die lands on a particular side (e.g. 6) more often than it should.
State the hypotheses

\(H_0\): The die is fair; \(p = 1/6\) (probability of that side).
\(H_a\): The die is “lucky” for that side; \(p > 1/6\).

Define errors in context

Type I error: Concluding that the die is “lucky” when it really isn’t.
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the die is “lucky” when it really is.

Define power

Power = probability of correctly concluding the die is lucky when it really is; Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

Balance the errors

Choose \(\alpha\) to limit false claims of a lucky die. Increase the number of rolls to improve power if we care about not missing a biased die.





Setup: A new medical treatment is studied. We want to know if it is effective in more than 10% of cases.
State the hypotheses

\(H_0\): The treatment is effective in exactly 10% of cases; \(p = 0.10\)
\(H_a\): The treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases; \(p > 0.10\).

Define errors in context

Type I error: Concluding that the treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases when it actually isn’t (false discovery — we might adopt an ineffective treatment).
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the treatment is effective when it actually helps more than 10% of patients (missed opportunity — we might abandon a beneficial treatment).

Define power

Power = probability of correctly concluding the treatment is effective when it really is (e.g. when true \(p > 0.10\)); Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

Balance the errors

If falsely approving an ineffective treatment is very serious, use a smaller \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.01). If missing a beneficial treatment is very serious, design the study with larger sample size (and possibly a somewhat larger \(\alpha\)) to achieve higher power.

Increasing power

Increase the sample size; optionally use a slightly larger \(\alpha\) if Type II error is the main concern; or focus the alternative (e.g. a specific \(p > 0.10\)) to design for that effect size.












Term
Meaning




Null hypothesis (\(H_0\))
The null model; a statement of no effect or no difference; the default assumption (e.g. “innocence”).


Alternative hypothesis (\(H_a\))
The statement we consider if we reject \(H_0\) (e.g. there is an effect or a difference).


P-value
The probability of observing data as or more extreme than what we saw, if the null model were true. Small p-value = strong evidence against \(H_0\); large p-value = we cannot confidently reject \(H_0\).


Type I error
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (false alarm).


Type II error
Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false (missed opportunity).


Power
Probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false; Power = 1 − P(Type II error).



Use the trial-by-jury analogy: \(H_0\) = default assumption of innocence, p-value = weight of evidence, and the Decision vs. Reality table to see how Type I, Type II, and power fit together.

P-value = the weight of the evidence against the null. A small p-value means the data would be unlikely if \(H_0\) were true — strong evidence against “innocence.” A large p-value means the data are plausible under \(H_0\) — we do not have enough evidence to reject the default.
Reject \(H_0\) = like a guilty verdict: we conclude the evidence is strong enough to reject the default (innocence).
Fail to reject \(H_0\) = like a not-guilty verdict: we do not have enough evidence to reject the default; we are not saying \(H_0\) is “true,” only that we cannot reject it.

title: Lecture 17
text:
The usual flow:

State the hypotheses: \(H_0\) (null, default) and \(H_a\) (alternative).
Collect data and compute a test statistic (e.g. sample proportion, count).
Compute the p-value: the probability of observing data as or more extreme than what we saw, if \(H_0\) were true.
Decide: If the p-value is small (e.g. below a chosen significance level \(\alpha\)), reject \(H_0\); otherwise fail to reject \(H_0\).

Every decision is made in the face of uncertainty: \(H_0\) might really be true or false, and we might reject it or not. So we can make mistaken decisions.

title: Lecture 17
text:
Our decision (reject or fail to reject \(H_0\)) can be right or wrong depending on reality (whether \(H_0\) is actually true or false):









Reality: \(H_0\) true
Reality: \(H_0\) false




Decision: Reject \(H_0\)
Type I error (false alarm)
Correct — we reject when we should (power)


Decision: Fail to reject \(H_0\)
Correct — we keep the default when it’s true
Type II error (missed opportunity)




Type I error: Rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true.
Type II error: Failing to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually false.
Power: Probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. So Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

title: Lecture 17
text:
Type I error is a “false alarm”: we conclude there is an effect (reject the null) when there isn’t. In the trial analogy, we convict an innocent person.
Type II error is a “missed opportunity”: we fail to conclude there is an effect when there really is one. In the trial analogy, we fail to convict someone who is guilty.

We cannot make both error probabilities arbitrarily small at once with a fixed sample size. So we balance them: choose a significance level \(\alpha\) (max acceptable Type I error) and then try to achieve reasonable power (low Type II error) via sample size or design.

title: Lecture 17
text:
Type I error: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true. We control this with the significance level \(\alpha\) (e.g. \(\alpha = 0.05\)): we reject \(H_0\) only when the p-value \(< \alpha\).
Type II error: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false. Its probability depends on the true state of nature (e.g. how far the true \(p\) is from the null value), the sample size, and \(\alpha\).
Power: The probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. Power = 1 − P(Type II error). Higher power means we are more likely to detect a real effect when it exists.

title: Lecture 17
text:
When Type I error is more serious (e.g. approving a harmful drug): use a smaller \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.01) so we require stronger evidence (smaller p-value) before rejecting \(H_0\).
When Type II error is more serious (e.g. missing a beneficial treatment): we want higher power. We can increase power by increasing the sample size or, if acceptable, using a somewhat larger \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.10).
Increasing sample size generally increases power (reduces Type II error) without changing the Type I error rate \(\alpha\).

title: Lecture 17
text:
Setup: We want to know if a coin lands on heads more often than not (a “lucky” coin for heads).
State the hypotheses

\(H_0\): The coin is fair; \(p = 0.5\) (probability of heads).
\(H_a\): The coin is lucky for heads; \(p > 0.5\).

Define errors in context

Type I error: Concluding that the coin is “lucky” when it actually is not (false alarm).
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the coin is “lucky” when it actually is (missed opportunity).

Define power

Power = probability of correctly concluding the coin is lucky when it really is (\(p > 0.5\)); i.e. Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

Balance the errors

Choose \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.05) to control how often we falsely declare the coin lucky. To reduce the chance of missing a truly lucky coin, increase the number of flips (sample size) to increase power.

title: Lecture 17
text:
Setup: We want to know if a die lands on a particular side (e.g. 6) more often than it should.
State the hypotheses

\(H_0\): The die is fair; \(p = 1/6\) (probability of that side).
\(H_a\): The die is “lucky” for that side; \(p > 1/6\).

Define errors in context

Type I error: Concluding that the die is “lucky” when it really isn’t.
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the die is “lucky” when it really is.

Define power

Power = probability of correctly concluding the die is lucky when it really is; Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

Balance the errors

Choose \(\alpha\) to limit false claims of a lucky die. Increase the number of rolls to improve power if we care about not missing a biased die.

title: Lecture 17
text:
Setup: A new medical treatment is studied. We want to know if it is effective in more than 10% of cases.
State the hypotheses

\(H_0\): The treatment is effective in exactly 10% of cases; \(p = 0.10\)
\(H_a\): The treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases; \(p > 0.10\).

Define errors in context

Type I error: Concluding that the treatment is effective in more than 10% of cases when it actually isn’t (false discovery — we might adopt an ineffective treatment).
Type II error: Failing to conclude that the treatment is effective when it actually helps more than 10% of patients (missed opportunity — we might abandon a beneficial treatment).

Define power

Power = probability of correctly concluding the treatment is effective when it really is (e.g. when true \(p > 0.10\)); Power = 1 − P(Type II error).

Balance the errors

If falsely approving an ineffective treatment is very serious, use a smaller \(\alpha\) (e.g. 0.01). If missing a beneficial treatment is very serious, design the study with larger sample size (and possibly a somewhat larger \(\alpha\)) to achieve higher power.

Increasing power

Increase the sample size; optionally use a slightly larger \(\alpha\) if Type II error is the main concern; or focus the alternative (e.g. a specific \(p > 0.10\)) to design for that effect size.

title: Lecture 17
text:
Term
Meaning




Null hypothesis (\(H_0\))
The null model; a statement of no effect or no difference; the default assumption (e.g. “innocence”).


Alternative hypothesis (\(H_a\))
The statement we consider if we reject \(H_0\) (e.g. there is an effect or a difference).


P-value
The probability of observing data as or more extreme than what we saw, if the null model were true. Small p-value = strong evidence against \(H_0\); large p-value = we cannot confidently reject \(H_0\).


Type I error
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (false alarm).


Type II error
Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false (missed opportunity).


Power
Probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false; Power = 1 − P(Type II error).



Use the trial-by-jury analogy: \(H_0\) = default assumption of innocence, p-value = weight of evidence, and the Decision vs. Reality table to see how Type I, Type II, and power fit together.

title: Lecture 16
text:
Use the CLT to approximate the Binomial Distribution with the Normal Distribution.
State the Empirical Rule for the normal distribution.
Draw a rejection region by hand to test a null hypothesis for one proportion/probability p.





When \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)\) with \(n\) large enough, the distribution of \(X\) is approximately normal with the same mean and standard deviation:

Mean: \(\mu = n p\)
Standard deviation: \(\sigma = \sqrt{n\,p\,(1-p)}\)

So we can approximate \(P(X \leq x)\) or \(P(X \geq x)\) using a normal curve with that \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).
Conditions for the approximation to be reasonable: Both the expected number of successes and the expected number of failures under the null are at least 10:

\(n\,p \geq 10\)
\(n\,(1-p) \geq 10\)

This keeps the binomial from being too skewed so the normal curve fits reasonably well.




For a normal distribution with mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\):

About 68% of the distribution lies within 1 standard deviation of the mean: between \(\mu - \sigma\) and \(\mu + \sigma\).
About 95% lies within 2 standard deviations: between \(\mu - 2\sigma\) and \(\mu + 2\sigma\).
About 99.7% lies within 3 standard deviations: between \(\mu - 3\sigma\) and \(\mu + 3\sigma\).





Use the One Proportion applet to see the binomial distribution and its normal approximation
One Proportion applet
Try different values of \(n\) and \(p\): when \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\), the normal curve overlays the binomial bars closely. When those conditions fail, the binomial is skewed and the normal fit is poor.




To test a null hypothesis about a proportion (e.g. \(H_0: p = 0.5\) for a fair coin):

Under \(H_0\), identify \(n\) and \(p\); compute \(\mu = np\) and \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\).
Sketch a normal curve centered at \(\mu\) with spread \(\sigma\) (or mark \(\mu\), \(\mu \pm \sigma\), \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\) using the Empirical Rule).
Mark the observed value of \(x\) (the count) on the horizontal axis.
Shade the rejection region: the tail(s) in the direction of \(H_a\) (e.g. for \(H_a: p > 0.5\), shade the right tail; for \(H_a: p \neq 0.5\), shade both tails).
The p-value is the area of the shaded region under the normal curve (approximate). If that area is small, reject \(H_0\); if it is large, fail to reject \(H_0\).





Research question: Is the coin fair? We test \(H_0: p = 0.5\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 0.5\), where \(p\) = probability of heads on a single flip.
Data: We flip the coin 100 times and observe 35 heads.

\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 100\), \(p = 0.5\).
Check conditions: \(np = 50 \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) = 50 \geq 10\). OK to use the normal approximation.
Mean and SD: \(\mu = 100 \times 0.5 = 50\), \(\sigma = \sqrt{100 \times 0.5 \times 0.5} = 5\).
Observed: \(x = 35\) heads. Under a normal curve with mean 50 and SD 5, 35 is 3 standard deviations below the mean (\(z = (35 - 50)/5 = -3\)). The Empirical Rule says only about 0.15% of the distribution is below \(\mu - 3\sigma\), so the left tail (and the symmetric right tail for a two-sided test) is very small.
Rejection region: Shade both tails (since \(H_a: p \neq 0.5\)). The observed 35 falls far into the left tail.
p-value: The area in the left tail (and the symmetric right tail) is very small. So the p-value is small.
Conclusion: Reject \(H_0\). The data are inconsistent with a fair coin; we have evidence that \(p \neq 0.5\) (here, fewer heads than expected).





Research question: Is the die fair for the face “6”? We test \(H_0: p = 1/6\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 1/6\), where \(p\) = probability of a 6 on a single roll.
Data: We roll the die 60 times and observe 12 sixes.

\(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\): \(n = 60\), \(p = 1/6\).
Check conditions: \(np = 60 \times (1/6) = 10 \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) = 60 \times (5/6) = 50 \geq 10\). OK to use the normal approximation.
Mean and SD: \(\mu = 60 \times (1/6) = 10\), \(\sigma = \sqrt{60 \times (1/6) \times (5/6)} = \sqrt{50/6} \approx 2.89\).
Observed: \(x = 12\) sixes. Under a normal curve with mean 10 and SD 2.89, \(z = (12 - 10)/2.89 \approx 0.69\). So 12 is a bit above the mean, within about one standard deviation. Most of the distribution lies between \(\mu - 2\sigma\) and \(\mu + 2\sigma\) (about 95%); 12 is well inside that range.
Rejection region: For \(H_a: p \neq 1/6\), we would shade both tails. The observed 12 is not in the tails; it’s near the center.
p-value: The area in the tails (values as or more extreme than 12) is large — 12 is a typical outcome when \(p = 1/6\).
Conclusion: Fail to reject \(H_0\). The data are consistent with a fair die; we do not have evidence that \(p \neq 1/6\).



















Example
\(H_0\)
\(n\)
Observed \(x\)
\(\mu\)
\(\sigma\)
Location of \(x\)
p-value
Conclusion




Coin
\(p = 0.5\)
100
35 heads
50
5
Far in left tail
Small
Reject \(H_0\)


Die
\(p = 1/6\)
60
12 sixes
10
\(\approx 2.89\)
Near center
Large
Fail to reject \(H_0\)



Use the One Proportion applet to enter these \(n\) and \(p\), see the normal overlay, and confirm the rejection regions and conclusions.

If you don’t have ESP, you’re guessing among 5 symbols, so \(p = 0.2\). If you do have ESP, \(p > 0.2\).

title: Lecture 6
text:
Lecture 6: Conditional Probability & Multiplication Rule
Learning Outcomes:

State the definition of conditional probability and compute \(P(A \mid B)\).
Use the multiplication rule to compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Determine whether two events are independent (using equivalent criteria).


Conditional Probability
Notation: \(P(A \mid B)\) means “the probability event \(A\) occurs given event \(B\) occurred.”
Definition: For events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\),
\[
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}
\]



Example 1 (die roll): Consider the trial of rolling a fair six-sided die once.
Let \(A\) be the event “the roll is a 3” and let \(B\) be the event “the roll is odd.”

List the outcomes in \(A\), in \(B\), and in \(A \text{ and } B\).
Compute \(P(A)\), \(P(B)\), and \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Find \(P(A \mid B)\): the probability of getting a 3 given the roll resulted in an odd number.
Find \(P(B \mid A)\): the probability of getting an odd number given the roll resulted in a 3.




Practice Problem 1: A jar contains 10 candies: 6 are red and 4 are blue. Two candies are selected without replacement.
Let \(A\) be the event “the first candy is red” and \(B\) be the event “the second candy is red.”

Find \(P(A)\).
Find \(P(B \mid A)\).
Use the multiplication rule (below) to find \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).





Multiplication Rule
For any two events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\),
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B)
\]
Equivalently (if \(P(A) > 0\)),
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)
\]



Example 2 (two cards, no replacement): A card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck, then a second card is drawn without replacement.
Find the probability the first card is an Ace and the second card is a King.




Independence
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other.
Equivalent ways to check independence:

\(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\) (when \(P(B) > 0\))
\(P(B \mid A) = P(B)\) (when \(P(A) > 0\))
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)\)




Example 3 (king vs spade): Suppose a card is randomly dealt from a standard 52-card deck.
Is the event “the card is a king” independent of the event “the card is a spade”?




Multiplication Rule for Two Independent Events
If \(A\) and \(B\) are independent, then
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A)\,P(B)
\]



Example 4 (two coin tosses): Suppose a fair coin is tossed twice.
Find the probability of getting “heads” on the first toss and “tails” on the second toss.



Practice Problem 2: Suppose a fair six-sided die is rolled twice.
Let \(A\) be the event “the first roll is even” and let \(B\) be the event “the second roll is 6.”

Are \(A\) and \(B\) independent? (Explain briefly.)
Compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).

title: Lecture 22
text:
Compute a z-score given an observation, and the mean and standard deviation of a population.
Interpret a z-score in context
Use z-scores to perform standardized comparisons
Compute the probability of a z-score or below using the Standard Normal Distribution and interpret it as the proportion of values below.

Example: Who is taller, Rosanna or Peter?
Two observations:
Rosanna’s height in inches is ________________
Peter’s height in inches is _________________
So ___________ is taller when their heights are compared directly.
But who is taller relative to their group?
Draw a reference distribution for the stature of men and a reference distribution for the stature of women.
Identify the location of the center of the stature distribution on each plot and show one and two standard deviations (SD) on either side.
Add vertical lines to the appropriate distribution at Rosanna and Peter’s heights. Who appears to be taller compared with the reference distributions?
According to the plots, _____________ is taller relative to their group.
Instead of plotting two different reference distribution and comparing the observations where they fall relative to two SD from the center, we can compare the heights relative to their groups directly via z-scores.



\[
z = \frac{\text{observation} - \text{mean of reference}}{\text{SD of reference}}
\]
A z-score measures the number of SDs the observation is above the mean (or below if the difference is negative).
Compute the z-score for Rosanna’s height using the reference information for women. Compute the z-score for Peter’s z-score using the reference information for men. Whose z-score is larger, that is, who is taller than more of their reference group?
If we assume the shape of a reference distribution is Normal then we can use a table or online calculator or R to find the percentage in the population that corresponds to a particular z-score.



A standard normal distribution is a normal distribution with mean 0 and SD 1.
Notice that by the Empirical Rule, we can see that 68% of values are between -1 and 1, 95% are between -2 and 2, and 99% are between -3 and 3. The proportions of values corresponding to all those below a particular value are given in a Standard Normal Table:
normal-table.pdf
Online Calculator
Example: Rosanna’s grip strength this morning using her dominant hand was 38.8 lbs. Suppose that mean grip strength is 66lbs and SD is 14lbs for women in their early forties in the US. Compute Rosanna’s z-score and use it to find the percentage of women in their early forties in the US that Rosanna is stronger than assuming that this distribution is normal.
Example: Measure your own grip strength in lbs: ___________________________________________
Compare with your choice of a reference group by computing your z-score.

title: Lab 3
text:
Purpose: This lab uses simulation in R to estimate probabilities involving conditional probability and the multiplication rule. We build on Lab 1–2 skills: sample(), for loops, if statements, vectors, and mean().
Topics:

Definition of conditional probability
Multiplication rule: \(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)\)
Simulation with if statements to count successes
Simulating populations with conditional structure

R functions you’ll use: rep(), sample(), for loop, if statement, vector(), sum(), mean(), which()



A bag contains 3 red and 3 white balls. Two balls are drawn one at a time; the first ball is not replaced before the second is drawn.



Your task: Run code to simulate 10 sets of two draws from the bag. Use "R" and "W" for red and white. Store a 1 in successes[k] when the first draw is white and the second is red.




Your task: Describe what the vector balls contains. How would you modify it for a bag with 5 red and 2 white balls?




Your task: What does the condition in the if statement specify? When is a “success” recorded?




Your task: Use which() to find which replicate numbers had a success.




Your task: Set replicates <- 10000 and re-run the simulation. Estimate \(P(\text{white first and red second})\).




Your task: Modify the simulation to estimate \(P(\text{exactly one red})\) — i.e., either (R then W) or (W then R). Use the logical operator | for “or”.





In the US, suppose about 20% of children and 3% of adults like to eat gummy vitamins. Assume a 1:1 child to adult ratio.
Let \(C\) = child, \(G\) = like to eat gummy vitamins.
Questions:

What is \(P(C \text{ and } G)\)?
What is \(P(G)\)?

R functions you’ll use: sample() with prob, for loop, if statement, sum(), mean()



Your task: Create vectors child (0 = no, 1 = yes) and gummy (0 = don’t like gummies, 1 = likes gummies). Use if statements so that the probability of liking gummies depends on being a child or not.




Your task: From the simulated population, estimate the probability of being a child and liking gummies, and the probability of liking gummies (overall).





A disease has prevalence \(P(D) = 0.01\). A test has sensitivity \(P(\text{positive} \mid D) = 0.95\) and specificity \(P(\text{negative} \mid \text{no } D) = 0.90\).
Your task: Simulate 10,000 individuals with disease status and test result. Estimate \(P(D \mid \text{positive})\) — the probability of having the disease given a positive test.






## Part 4: ReflectionYour task: In 2–3 sentences, describe what you learned from this lab about coding in R and conditional probabilities. Were there any questions you couldn’t figure out how to write the code for?

title: Lab 8
text:
Topics: Lectures 19–20 — Confidence intervals, sampling methods (convenience vs random), bias, and computing CIs in R (Wald, Plus Four, FPC for proportion and mean).



Placeholder: Material to be added.
Use the Gettysburg Address activity from Rossman Chance to compare convenience sampling (e.g. taking the first sentence or the first 30 words) with simple random sampling (e.g. randomly selecting 30 words from the full text). Compare estimates of a population parameter (e.g. mean word length, proportion of long words) and the impact on bias and generalizability.

Link or handout: [Rossman Chance applet/activity — to be inserted]
Questions: How do the two sampling methods differ? Which gives a more trustworthy estimate for the full address? Why?





Placeholder: R code to be added.
Provide R code that computes 95% confidence intervals using all five methods covered in the course:

Proportion — Wald (z-interval): \(\hat{p} \pm 2\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}\) (or equivalent R function).
Proportion — Plus Four: \(\tilde{p} = (x+2)/(n+4)\), then \(\tilde{p} \pm 2\sqrt{\tilde{p}(1-\tilde{p})/(n+4)}\).
Proportion — with finite population correction (FPC): Wald formula multiplied by \(\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}\).
Mean — normal (z) or t-interval: \(\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot s/\sqrt{n}\) (or t.test / equivalent).
Mean — with finite population correction (FPC): Mean interval multiplied by \(\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}\).

Use a single example dataset (e.g. St. Joe’s mortality or a small numeric vector) so students can compare the same data across methods where applicable.




Placeholder: Material to be added.
Show a sample Project 2 (or excerpt) so students can see the expected format, level of detail, and how to integrate sampling concepts with confidence interval interpretation.

Instructor: Insert the Gettysburg Address activity, R code chunks, and sample Project 2 in the sections above.

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
Goal: Start Lab 2 with a probability question on an equally likely sample space, then show how to compute it by counting and by simulation in R.


Before the 2D grid, we practice the same ideas on a 1D line of 5 sites.
We label sites as:
\[
S = \{1,2,3,4,5\}
\]
We choose 2 distinct sites without replacement, so the equally likely sample space is all 2-site sets:
\[
|S_2| = \binom{5}{2} = 10
\]


The “end sites” are 1 and 5.
Use the complement (“no ends” means both picks are from {2,3,4}):
\[
P(\text{at least one end})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{\binom{5}{2}}
= 1 - \frac{3}{10}
= \frac{7}{10}
\]



Adjacent pairs are:

{1,2}, {2,3}, {3,4}, {4,5} (4 pairs total)

So:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{4}{\binom{5}{2}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4
\]



set.seed(109)

sites <- 1:5
B <- 10000

at_least_one_end <- logical(B)
adjacent <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  draws <- sample(sites, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  a <- draws[1]
  c <- draws[2]

  # "at least one end" using the Lab 1 sum idea
  at_least_one_end[b] <- sum(c(a == 1, a == 5, c == 1, c == 5)) > 0

  # adjacent on a line if they differ by 1
  adjacent[b] <- (abs(a - c) == 1)
}

mean(at_least_one_end)
mean(adjacent)





We have an \(N \times N\) grid of possible sampling sites. Each site is equally likely.
For this demo, we will keep things realistic and simple:

We select 2 distinct sites without replacement from the grid.
Every unordered pair of sites is equally likely.

So the sample space size is:
\[
|S| = \binom{N^2}{2}
\]

Total sites: \(N^2\)
Corner sites: \(4\)
Edge sites (including corners): \(4N - 4\)

Define:

\(p_\text{corner} = \frac{4}{N^2}\)
\(p_\text{edge} = \frac{4N-4}{N^2}\)






Types

numeric vs integer vs character vs logical
class(x) (and optionally typeof(x))

Vectors

c(...) to create vectors
length(x) to check size
bracketing x[i] and bracketing with a logical vector x[x > 5]
element-by-element operations: x + 1, x * 2, x == y

Matrices

matrix(...) to create a matrix
dim(m) to check size
bracketing m[row, col], extracting rows/cols with m[1, ], m[, 2]

Simulation + loops

sample(..., replace = FALSE) (choose distinct sites)
%in% for “is it in this set?”
placeholders: logical(B), numeric(B)
for (b in 1:B) { ... } storing one result per run
probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE)




# Types
x_num <- 3.5
x_int <- 3L
x_chr <- "site"
x_log <- (3 < 5)

class(x_num); class(x_int); class(x_chr); class(x_log)

# Vector + length + scalar operations
v <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
length(v)
v + 1
v * 2

# Matrix + dim + bracketing
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
dim(m)
m[2, 2]
m[1, ]
m[, 2]





We are choosing 2 distinct sites from \(N^2\) equally likely sites.
There are 4 corners and \(N^2 - 4\) non-corners.
Use the complement (“no corners”):
\[
P(\text{at least one corner})
= 1 - P(\text{no corners})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{N^2 - 4}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]




Edges (including corners) total \(4N - 4\) sites, so non-edges total:
\[
N^2 - (4N - 4) = (N-2)^2
\]
Use the complement (“no edges”):
\[
P(\text{at least one edge})
= 1 - P(\text{no edges})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{(N-2)^2}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]




We will say two sites are adjacent if they are 4-neighbors (up/down/left/right).


In an \(N\times N\) grid:

Horizontal adjacent pairs: \(N(N-1)\)
Vertical adjacent pairs: \(N(N-1)\)

So total adjacent (unordered) pairs:
\[
2N(N-1)
\]
Total possible unordered pairs of sites:
\[
\binom{N^2}{2}
\]
Therefore:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{2N(N-1)}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]





Pick values that are easy to talk through in class. For example:

\(N = 5\) (a \(5\times 5\) grid, 25 sites)

Then:

Total pairs: \(\binom{25}{2} = 300\)
Adjacent pairs: \(2N(N-1)=2\cdot 5\cdot 4=40\)

We can compute the three probabilities in R.
N <- 5
total_pairs <- choose(N^2, 2)

# 1) at least one corner
p_at_least_one_corner <- 1 - choose(N^2 - 4, 2) / total_pairs

# 2) at least one edge
p_at_least_one_edge <- 1 - choose((N - 2)^2, 2) / total_pairs

# 3) adjacent
p_adjacent <- (2 * N * (N - 1)) / total_pairs

p_at_least_one_corner
p_at_least_one_edge
p_adjacent




We simulate \(B\) random samples of 2 distinct sites, and estimate probabilities by proportions (mean(TRUE/FALSE)).
We label each grid cell with a string "i,j" so the sampled sites look like coordinates you’d write by hand.
New-to-Lab-1/2 note: this uses paste() to create "i,j" labels and strsplit() + as.numeric() to turn "i,j" back into numbers for the adjacency check.
set.seed(109)

N <- 5
B <- 10000

# Make a grid whose entries look like "i,j" (row,col)
grid <- matrix("", nrow = N, ncol = N)
for (i in 1:N) {
  for (j in 1:N) {
    grid[i, j] <- paste(i, j, sep = ",")
  }
}

corners <- c(grid[1,1], grid[1,N], grid[N,1], grid[N,N])
edges   <- c(grid[1, ], grid[N, ], grid[2:(N-1), 1], grid[2:(N-1), N])

at_least_one_corner <- logical(B)
at_least_one_edge   <- logical(B)
adjacent            <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  picks <- sample(grid, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  s1 <- picks[1]
  s2 <- picks[2]

  rc1 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s1, ",")[[1]])
  rc2 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s2, ",")[[1]])

  r1 <- rc1[1]; c1 <- rc1[2]
  r2 <- rc2[1]; c2 <- rc2[2]

  # keep the Lab 1 "sum of TRUE/FALSE" idea (avoid new | and & operators)
  at_least_one_corner[b] <- sum(c(s1 %in% corners, s2 %in% corners)) > 0
  at_least_one_edge[b]   <- sum(c(s1 %in% edges,   s2 %in% edges))   > 0

  same_row_adj <- (r1 == r2) * (abs(c1 - c2) == 1)
  same_col_adj <- (c1 == c2) * (abs(r1 - r2) == 1)
  adjacent[b] <- sum(c(same_row_adj, same_col_adj)) > 0
}

mean(at_least_one_corner)
mean(at_least_one_edge)
mean(adjacent)





Equally likely sample space: every 2-site sample (without replacement) is equally likely.
Counting: corners = 4; edges = \(4N-4\); total sites = \(N^2\); total pairs = \(\binom{N^2}{2}\).
Matrices + bracketing: grid <- matrix("", ...), corners <- c(grid[1,1], ...), etc.
For loop: pre-allocate storage (logical(B)) and store one result per iteration.
Probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE) gives a proportion.

placeholders: logical(B), numeric(B)
for (b in 1:B) { ... } storing one result per run
probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE)




# Types
x_num <- 3.5
x_int <- 3L
x_chr <- "site"
x_log <- (3 < 5)

class(x_num); class(x_int); class(x_chr); class(x_log)

# Vector + length + scalar operations
v <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
length(v)
v + 1
v * 2

# Matrix + dim + bracketing
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
dim(m)
m[2, 2]
m[1, ]
m[, 2]

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
We are choosing 2 distinct sites from \(N^2\) equally likely sites.
There are 4 corners and \(N^2 - 4\) non-corners.
Use the complement (“no corners”):
\[
P(\text{at least one corner})
= 1 - P(\text{no corners})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{N^2 - 4}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
Edges (including corners) total \(4N - 4\) sites, so non-edges total:
\[
N^2 - (4N - 4) = (N-2)^2
\]
Use the complement (“no edges”):
\[
P(\text{at least one edge})
= 1 - P(\text{no edges})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{(N-2)^2}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
We will say two sites are adjacent if they are 4-neighbors (up/down/left/right).


In an \(N\times N\) grid:

Horizontal adjacent pairs: \(N(N-1)\)
Vertical adjacent pairs: \(N(N-1)\)

So total adjacent (unordered) pairs:
\[
2N(N-1)
\]
Total possible unordered pairs of sites:
\[
\binom{N^2}{2}
\]
Therefore:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{2N(N-1)}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
Pick values that are easy to talk through in class. For example:

\(N = 5\) (a \(5\times 5\) grid, 25 sites)

Then:

Total pairs: \(\binom{25}{2} = 300\)
Adjacent pairs: \(2N(N-1)=2\cdot 5\cdot 4=40\)

We can compute the three probabilities in R.
N <- 5
total_pairs <- choose(N^2, 2)

# 1) at least one corner
p_at_least_one_corner <- 1 - choose(N^2 - 4, 2) / total_pairs

# 2) at least one edge
p_at_least_one_edge <- 1 - choose((N - 2)^2, 2) / total_pairs

# 3) adjacent
p_adjacent <- (2 * N * (N - 1)) / total_pairs

p_at_least_one_corner
p_at_least_one_edge
p_adjacent

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
We simulate \(B\) random samples of 2 distinct sites, and estimate probabilities by proportions (mean(TRUE/FALSE)).
We label each grid cell with a string "i,j" so the sampled sites look like coordinates you’d write by hand.
New-to-Lab-1/2 note: this uses paste() to create "i,j" labels and strsplit() + as.numeric() to turn "i,j" back into numbers for the adjacency check.
set.seed(109)

N <- 5
B <- 10000

# Make a grid whose entries look like "i,j" (row,col)
grid <- matrix("", nrow = N, ncol = N)
for (i in 1:N) {
  for (j in 1:N) {
    grid[i, j] <- paste(i, j, sep = ",")
  }
}

corners <- c(grid[1,1], grid[1,N], grid[N,1], grid[N,N])
edges   <- c(grid[1, ], grid[N, ], grid[2:(N-1), 1], grid[2:(N-1), N])

at_least_one_corner <- logical(B)
at_least_one_edge   <- logical(B)
adjacent            <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  picks <- sample(grid, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  s1 <- picks[1]
  s2 <- picks[2]

  rc1 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s1, ",")[[1]])
  rc2 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s2, ",")[[1]])

  r1 <- rc1[1]; c1 <- rc1[2]
  r2 <- rc2[1]; c2 <- rc2[2]

  # keep the Lab 1 "sum of TRUE/FALSE" idea (avoid new | and & operators)
  at_least_one_corner[b] <- sum(c(s1 %in% corners, s2 %in% corners)) > 0
  at_least_one_edge[b]   <- sum(c(s1 %in% edges,   s2 %in% edges))   > 0

  same_row_adj <- (r1 == r2) * (abs(c1 - c2) == 1)
  same_col_adj <- (c1 == c2) * (abs(r1 - r2) == 1)
  adjacent[b] <- sum(c(same_row_adj, same_col_adj)) > 0
}

mean(at_least_one_corner)
mean(at_least_one_edge)
mean(adjacent)

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
Equally likely sample space: every 2-site sample (without replacement) is equally likely.
Counting: corners = 4; edges = \(4N-4\); total sites = \(N^2\); total pairs = \(\binom{N^2}{2}\).
Matrices + bracketing: grid <- matrix("", ...), corners <- c(grid[1,1], ...), etc.
For loop: pre-allocate storage (logical(B)) and store one result per iteration.
Probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE) gives a proportion.

title: Lab 2
text:
This lab introduces data structures in R (types, vectors, matrices), bracketing, and for loops, with a short probability simulation component.



Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text: Purpose: This lab builds on Labs 1–4 and Lectures 15–16. You will test hypotheses about a proportion using a binomial model (exact) and the normal approximation (Lecture 16). You will compare when the approximation is poor vs good (conditions \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\)).

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text:
Learning goals

Test \(H_0: p = p_0\) vs \(H_a: p \neq p_0\) (two-sided) for a binomial random process.
Plot the null distribution with dbinom and geom_col; add vertical line at the data and shade the p-value region.
Compute the two-sided p-value with pbinom.
Compute center \(\mu = np\) and spread \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\); add labeled vertical lines on the binomial plot.
Plot the normal approximation (same x-axis): dnorm with mean \(np\), sd \(\sqrt{np(1-p)}\); add vertical lines at \(\mu\), \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\), \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\), and a green line at the observed data.
Decide if the data fall in the rejection region (e.g. beyond 2 SDs from the mean) and state the conclusion.
Recognize when the normal approximation is poor vs good (conditions \(np \geq 10\), \(n(1-p) \geq 10\)).

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text:
Setup: load ggplot2
Run the cell below so you can use ggplot2 for plotting.
Your task: Load the ggplot2 library.
Goal: No error; library loads.


Code
# Solution
library(ggplot2)

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text:
Problem 1 — Coin (small \(n\): poor normal approximation)
Scenario: We flip a coin 10 times and observe 1 head. Test whether the coin is fair: \(H_0: p = 0.5\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 0.5\) (two-sided).
Parts: (a) Set \(n\), \(p\), observed \(x\), and compute \(\mu\), \(\sigma\). Check \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\). (b) Binomial bar plot with vertical line at data and shaded two-sided p-value region. (c) Two-sided p-value using pbinom. (d) Add vertical lines at \(\mu\), \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\), \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\) (colored/labeled) on the binomial plot. (e) Normal approximation plot (same x scale): dnorm curve, vertical lines at mean and \(\pm 1\sigma\), \(\pm 2\sigma\), and green line at the data. (f) Does the observed value fall in the rejection region (e.g. beyond 2 SDs)? Conclusion?

1(a) — Set parameters and compute center and spread
Your task: Define n, p, and the observed count x_obs. Compute mu = n*p and sigma = sqrt(n*p*(1-p)). Check whether n*p >= 10 and n*(1-p) >= 10 (store as logicals or print).
Goal output: Numbers for mu, sigma, and whether the approximation conditions hold.


Code
# Solution — Problem 1
n <- 10
p <- 0.5
x_obs <- 1
mu <- n * p
sigma <- sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
cat("mu =", mu, "  sigma =", sigma, "\n")
cat("np >= 10?", n*p >= 10, "  n(1-p) >= 10?", n*(1-p) >= 10, "\n")




1(b) — Binomial bar plot with vertical line at data and shaded p-value region
Your task: Build a data frame of x = 0:n and prob = dbinom(x, n, p). For the two-sided p-value, shade the bars that are in the “as or more extreme” region (both tails). Use geom_col() and add a vertical line at x_obs. Option: add a column in_tail that is TRUE for bars to shade (e.g. x <= x_obs or x >= cutoff for the other tail), then use geom_col(aes(fill = in_tail)).
Goal: Bar plot of null distribution with observed value marked and both tails shaded.


Code
# Solution — Problem 1(b)
x_vals <- 0:n
df_binom <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
# Two-sided: shade both tails (smaller tail doubled).
p_lo <- pbinom(x_obs, n, p)
p_hi <- 1 - pbinom(x_obs - 1, n, p)
p_tail <- min(p_lo, p_hi)
# Right-tail cutoff so that P(X >= x_high) = p_tail
x_high <- qbinom(1 - p_tail, n, p) + 1
df_binom$in_tail <- (df_binom$x <= x_obs) | (df_binom$x >= x_high)

ggplot(df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob, fill = in_tail)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1, color = "green3") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)", title = "Problem 1: Binomial null (n=10, p=0.5)") +
  theme_minimal()




1(c) — Two-sided p-value using pbinom
Your task: Compute the two-sided p-value as 2 * min(P(X <= x_obs), P(X >= x_obs)) using pbinom. Print the result.
Goal output: A single number (the p-value).


Code
# Solution — Problem 1(c)
p_lo <- pbinom(x_obs, n, p)
p_hi <- 1 - pbinom(x_obs - 1, n, p)
p_value_2sided <- 2 * min(p_lo, p_hi)
cat("Two-sided p-value =", p_value_2sided, "\n")




1(d) — Add vertical lines at mean and ±1 SD, ±2 SD on the binomial plot
Your task: On the same binomial bar plot (or a new one), add vertical lines at mu, mu - sigma, mu + sigma, mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma with different colors and labels (e.g. “μ”, “μ−σ”, “μ+σ”, “μ−2σ”, “μ+2σ”).
Goal: Bar plot with center and spread marked.


Code
# Solution — Problem 1(d)
ggplot(df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob, fill = in_tail)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red", linetype = "solid") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 4) +
  annotate("text", x = mu - sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.85, label = "μ−σ", color = "orange", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = mu + sigma, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.85, label = "μ+σ", color = "orange", size = 3) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)", title = "Problem 1: Binomial with center and spread") +
  theme_minimal()




1(e) — Normal approximation plot (same x scale)
Your task: Create a plot with the same x-axis (0 to n). Draw the normal curve: dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma) over a fine grid of x values. Add vertical lines at mu, mu ± sigma, mu ± 2*sigma (labeled), and a green vertical line at x_obs.
Goal: Normal curve with mean, ±1 SD, ±2 SD, and observed value marked.


Code
# Solution — Problem 1(e)
x_fine <- seq(0, n, by = 0.05)
df_norm <- data.frame(x = x_fine, y = dnorm(x_fine, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

ggplot(df_norm, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1.2, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 4) +
  annotate("text", x = mu - sigma, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.8, label = "μ−σ", color = "orange", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = mu + sigma, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.8, label = "μ+σ", color = "orange", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = mu - 2*sigma, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.65, label = "μ−2σ", color = "purple", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = mu + 2*sigma, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.65, label = "μ+2σ", color = "purple", size = 3) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "density", title = "Problem 1: Normal approximation (poor: np, n(1-p) < 10)") +
  theme_minimal()




1(f) — Rejection region and conclusion
Your task: (Text answer.) Does the observed value (green line) fall beyond 2 SDs from the mean (i.e. in the rejection region)? State whether you reject or fail to reject \(H_0\) and a one-sentence conclusion in context.
Goal: Short written answer.
Solution — Problem 1(f): Yes. x_obs = 1. Here μ = 5 and σ ≈ 1.58, so μ−2σ ≈ 1.84. The value 1 is below μ−2σ (1 < 1.84), so the observed value falls in the rejection region (beyond 2 SDs below the mean). We reject H₀. Conclusion: The data are inconsistent with a fair coin; we have evidence that p ≠ 0.5 (too few heads).

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text:
Problem 2 — Die (small \(n\): poor normal approximation)
Scenario: We roll a die 12 times and observe 0 sixes. Test whether the die is fair for the face “6”: \(H_0: p = 1/6\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 1/6\) (two-sided).
Parts: (a)–(f) as in Problem 1: parameters, binomial plot with shading and vertical line at data, two-sided p-value, center/spread lines on binomial plot, normal approximation plot with green line at data, rejection region and conclusion.

2(a) — Parameters and center/spread


Code
# Solution — Problem 2
n <- 12
p <- 1/6
x_obs <- 0
mu <- n * p
sigma <- sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
cat("mu =", mu, "  sigma =", round(sigma, 4), "\n")
cat("np >= 10?", n*p >= 10, "  n(1-p) >= 10?", n*(1-p) >= 10, "\n")




2(b) — Binomial plot with shading and vertical line at data


Code
# Solution — Problem 2(b)
x_vals <- 0:n
df_binom <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
p_lo <- pbinom(x_obs, n, p)
p_hi <- 1 - pbinom(x_obs - 1, n, p)
p_tail <- min(p_lo, p_hi)
x_high <- qbinom(1 - p_tail, n, p) + 1
df_binom$in_tail <- (df_binom$x <= x_obs) | (df_binom$x >= x_high)

ggplot(df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob, fill = in_tail)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1, color = "green3") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)", title = "Problem 2: Binomial null (n=12, p=1/6)") +
  theme_minimal()




2(c) — Two-sided p-value


Code
# Solution — Problem 2(c)
p_lo <- pbinom(x_obs, n, p)
p_hi <- 1 - pbinom(x_obs - 1, n, p)
p_value_2sided <- 2 * min(p_lo, p_hi)
cat("Two-sided p-value =", round(p_value_2sided, 4), "\n")




2(d) — Binomial plot with center and spread lines


Code
# Solution — Problem 2(d)
ggplot(df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob, fill = in_tail)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_binom$prob)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 4) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)", title = "Problem 2: Binomial with center and spread") +
  theme_minimal()




2(e) — Normal approximation plot


Code
# Solution — Problem 2(e)
x_fine <- seq(0, n, by = 0.05)
df_norm <- data.frame(x = x_fine, y = dnorm(x_fine, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

ggplot(df_norm, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1.2, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 4) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "density", title = "Problem 2: Normal approximation (poor)") +
  theme_minimal()




2(f) — Rejection region and conclusion
Your task: Does the observed value fall beyond 2 SDs from the mean? Reject or fail to reject? Conclusion?
Solution — Problem 2(f): x_obs = 0. Here μ = 2, σ ≈ 1.29, so μ−2σ ≈ −0.58. The observed 0 is between μ−2σ and μ+2σ (within 2 SDs), so it does not fall in the rejection region. We fail to reject H₀. Conclusion: The data are consistent with a fair die (p = 1/6); we do not have evidence that p ≠ 1/6.

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text:
Problem 3 — Coin (large \(n\): good normal approximation)
Scenario: We flip a coin 50 times and observe 20 heads. Test \(H_0: p = 0.5\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 0.5\) (two-sided).
Parts: (a)–(f) as before. Here \(np = 25\) and \(n(1-p) = 25\), so the normal approximation is good.

3(a)–(f) — Full solution (parameters, binomial plot, p-value, center/spread, normal plot, conclusion)


Code
# Solution — Problem 3
n <- 50
p <- 0.5
x_obs <- 20
mu <- n * p
sigma <- sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
cat("mu =", mu, "  sigma =", sigma, "\n")
cat("np >= 10?", n*p >= 10, "  n(1-p) >= 10?", n*(1-p) >= 10, "\n")

p_lo <- pbinom(x_obs, n, p)
p_hi <- 1 - pbinom(x_obs - 1, n, p)
p_value_2sided <- 2 * min(p_lo, p_hi)
cat("Two-sided p-value =", round(p_value_2sided, 4), "\n")




Code
# Binomial plot with shading and center/spread
x_vals <- 0:n
df_binom <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
p_tail <- min(p_lo, p_hi)
x_high <- qbinom(1 - p_tail, n, p) + 1
df_binom$in_tail <- (df_binom$x <= x_obs) | (df_binom$x >= x_high)

ggplot(df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob, fill = in_tail)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)", title = "Problem 3: Binomial (good approx)") +
  theme_minimal()




Code
# Normal approximation plot
x_fine <- seq(0, n, by = 0.1)
df_norm <- data.frame(x = x_fine, y = dnorm(x_fine, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

ggplot(df_norm, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1.2, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 4) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "density", title = "Problem 3: Normal approximation (good)") +
  theme_minimal()


Solution — Problem 3(f): x_obs = 20 is about 1 SD below μ (μ = 25, σ ≈ 3.54, so μ−σ ≈ 21.5). So 20 is inside [μ−2σ, μ+2σ] and does not fall in the rejection region. We fail to reject H₀. The data are consistent with a fair coin.

title: STAT 109 — Lab 6: Binomial Tests and Normal Approximation (Solutions)
text:
Problem 4 — Die (large \(n\): good normal approximation)
Scenario: We roll a die 60 times and observe 3 sixes. Test \(H_0: p = 1/6\) vs \(H_a: p \neq 1/6\) (two-sided).
Parts: (a)–(f). Here \(np = 10\) and \(n(1-p) = 50\), so the approximation is good. The observed 3 is well below the mean 10, so we expect to reject \(H_0\).


Code
# Solution — Problem 4
n <- 60
p <- 1/6
x_obs <- 3
mu <- n * p
sigma <- sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
cat("mu =", mu, "  sigma =", round(sigma, 4), "\n")
cat("np >= 10?", n*p >= 10, "  n(1-p) >= 10?", n*(1-p) >= 10, "\n")

p_lo <- pbinom(x_obs, n, p)
p_hi <- 1 - pbinom(x_obs - 1, n, p)
p_value_2sided <- 2 * min(p_lo, p_hi)
cat("Two-sided p-value =", round(p_value_2sided, 4), "\n")




Code
# Binomial plot with shading and center/spread
x_vals <- 0:n
df_binom <- data.frame(x = x_vals, prob = dbinom(x_vals, n, p))
p_tail <- min(p_lo, p_hi)
x_high <- qbinom(1 - p_tail, n, p) + 1
df_binom$in_tail <- (df_binom$x <= x_obs) | (df_binom$x >= x_high)

ggplot(df_binom, aes(x = x, y = prob, fill = in_tail)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "x", y = "P(X = x)", title = "Problem 4: Binomial (good approx)") +
  theme_minimal()




Code
# Normal approximation plot
x_fine <- seq(0, n, by = 0.1)
df_norm <- data.frame(x = x_fine, y = dnorm(x_fine, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

ggplot(df_norm, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = x_obs, linewidth = 1.2, color = "green3") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linewidth = 0.8, color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - sigma, mu + sigma), linewidth = 0.6, color = "orange", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(mu - 2*sigma, mu + 2*sigma), linewidth = 0.5, color = "purple", linetype = "dotted") +
  annotate("text", x = mu, y = max(df_norm$y)*0.95, label = "μ", color = "red", size = 4) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "density", title = "Problem 4: Normal approximation (good)") +
  theme_minimal()


Solution — Problem 4(f): x_obs = 3. Here μ = 10, σ ≈ 2.89, so μ−2σ ≈ 4.2. The observed 3 is below μ−2σ, so it falls in the rejection region. We reject H₀. The data are inconsistent with a fair die (p = 1/6); we have evidence that p ≠ 1/6 (too few sixes).

Add Quiz 6 and images to git for publishing
Run these commands from the repository root (the folder that contains stat109-private):
# Add the quiz and the quiz-6 images folder
git add stat109-private/quizzes/quiz-6.qmd
git add stat109-private/assets/images/quiz-6/

# If you generated the PNGs with the R script, they're in that folder; add them too:
# (already included in the folder add above)

# Check what will be committed
git status

# Commit
git commit -m "Add Quiz 6 practice problems and null distribution images"
If the images (e.g. quiz6-fig1-left-handed.png) are not yet in stat109-private/assets/images/quiz-6/, generate them first:
& "C:\Program Files\R\R-4.5.2\bin\Rscript.exe" stat109-private/scripts/quiz6_plots.R
Then run the git add and git commit commands above. After that, push to your remote to publish (e.g. git push origin main).
